---
title: "Post-secondary pathways"
editor: visual
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE)
library(tidyverse)
library(sf)
library(ggrepel)
library(scales)
library(shiny)
library(shinycssloaders)
library(ggiraph)
library(kableExtra)
library(rmapshaper)
library(cowplot)
library(DT)
library(htmlwidgets)
library(RColorBrewer)
library(readxl)
library(janitor)
library(lubridate)
library(systemfonts)
reset_font_cache()
library(ggtext)
library(gmodels)
library(fastDummies)
library(car)
library(glmnet)
library(glmnetUtils)
library(pscl)
library(sjPlot)
library(ggforce)
library(openxlsx)
```

```{r themes and shapefiles, include=FALSE}
theme_bar <- theme_bw() +
  theme(panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey70", linewidth  = 0.1),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        axis.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
        panel.border = element_blank(),
        legend.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = "transparent"),
        legend.key = element_rect(fill = "transparent"),
        legend.key.size = unit(1, "lines"),
        legend.margin = margin(0,0,0,0),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.text = element_text(margin = margin(l = 2)),
        text = element_text(family = "Arial") ,
        plot.title.position = "plot",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

theme_line <- theme_bw() +
  theme(legend.background = element_rect(fill = "transparent", color = "transparent"),
        legend.key = element_rect(fill = "transparent"),
        legend.text = element_text(margin = margin(l = 2)),
        panel.grid.minor = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "grey70", linewidth = 0.1),
        axis.ticks = element_blank(),
        axis.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
        panel.border = element_blank(),
        legend.margin = margin(0,0,0,0),
        legend.key.size = unit(1, "lines"),
        text = element_text(family = "Arial") ,
        plot.title.position = "plot",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))


theme_sf <- theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.x=element_blank(),
        axis.text.y=element_blank(),
        axis.ticks=element_blank(),
        panel.background = element_blank(),
        panel.grid.major = element_line(color = "white"),
        panel.border = element_blank(),
        legend.title = element_blank(),
        legend.text = element_text(margin = margin(l = 2)),
        legend.margin = margin(0,0,0,0),
        legend.key.size = unit(1, "lines"),
        text = element_text(family = "Arial") ,
        plot.title.position = "plot",
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"))

regions <- read_csv("/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Data Prep/R Projects/Join docs/county_regions.csv") %>%
    select(5,6) %>%
    unique() %>%
    mutate(edr = str_replace(edr, "  ", " "),
           planning.region = str_replace(planning.region, " Minnesota", ""),
           planning.region = fct_relevel(planning.region, "Northwest", "Northeast", "Central", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul", "Southwest", "Southeast"),
           edr = fct_relevel(edr, "EDR 1 - Northwest", "EDR 2 - Headwaters", "EDR 3 - Arrowhead", "EDR 4 - West Central", "EDR 5 - North Central", "EDR 6E- Southwest Central", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley", "EDR 7E- East Central", "EDR 7W- Central", "EDR 8 - Southwest", "EDR 9 - South Central", "EDR 10 - Southeast", "EDR 11 - 7 County Twin Cities", "Minnesota"))

counties.regions <- read_csv("/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Data Prep/R Projects/Join docs/county_regions.csv") %>%
  rename(mif = `MIF Region`) %>%
  mutate(countyfp = formatC(countyfp, width = 3, flag = "0"),
         Name = str_to_title(Name),
         Name = str_replace(Name, "Q", "q"),
         Name = str_replace(Name, "Of The", "of the"),
         Name = str_replace(Name, "Mcleod", "McLeod"),
         Dem_Desc = ifelse(Name == "Minnesota", "Minnesota", Dem_Desc) ,
         edr = str_replace(edr, "  ", " "),
         planning.region = str_replace(planning.region, " Minnesota", ""),
         planning.region = fct_relevel(planning.region, "Northwest", "Northeast", "Central", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul", "Southwest", "Southeast"),
         edr = fct_relevel(edr, "EDR 1 - Northwest", "EDR 2 - Headwaters", "EDR 3 - Arrowhead", "EDR 4 - West Central", "EDR 5 - North Central", "EDR 6E- Southwest Central", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley", "EDR 7E- East Central", "EDR 7W- Central", "EDR 8 - Southwest", "EDR 9 - South Central", "EDR 10 - Southeast", "EDR 11 - 7 County Twin Cities", "Minnesota"),
         Dem_Desc = fct_relevel(Dem_Desc, "Entirely rural", "Town/rural mix", "Urban/town/rural mix", "Entirely urban"))

counties.regions.1 <- counties.regions %>%
  mutate(statefp = "27",
         project.pr = ifelse(edr %in% c("EDR 1 - Northwest", "EDR 2 - Headwaters", "EDR 4 - West Central"), "Northwest",
                             ifelse(edr == "EDR 3 - Arrowhead", "Northeast",
                                    ifelse(edr %in% c("EDR 5 - North Central", "EDR 7E- East Central", "EDR 7W- Central"), "Central",
                                           ifelse(edr %in% c("EDR 6E- Southwest Central", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley", "EDR 8 - Southwest"), "Southwest",
                                                  ifelse(edr %in% c("EDR 9 - South Central", "EDR 10 - Southeast"), "Southern", as.character(planning.region)))))),
         project.pr = fct_relevel(project.pr, "Northwest", "Northeast", "Central", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul", "Southwest", "Southern"))


color.ruca <- c("Entirely rural" = "#009933", "Town/rural mix" = "#99CC33", "Urban/town/rural mix" = "#CC9966", "Entirely urban" = "#754C29", "Minnesota" = "black")

color.pr <- c("Northwest" = 	"#4575b4", "Northeast" = "grey", "Central" = "#fee090", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul" = "#d73027", "Southwest" = "#91bfdb", "Southeast" = "#fc8d59", "Minnesota" = "black")

color.edr <- c("EDR 1 - Northwest" = "#b3cde3", "EDR 2 - Headwaters" = "#8c96c6", "EDR 3 - Arrowhead" = "#fe9929", "EDR 4 - West Central" = "#8856a7", "EDR 5 - North Central" = "#810f7c", "EDR 6E- Southwest Central" = "#e5f5f9", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley" = "#bdc9e1", "EDR 7E- East Central" = "#99d8c9", "EDR 7W- Central" = "#2ca25f", "EDR 8 - Southwest" = "#74a9cf", "EDR 9 - South Central" = "#0570b0", "EDR 10 - Southeast" = "#d7301f", "EDR 11 - 7 County Twin Cities" = "#d8b365", "Minnesota" = "black")

color.edr.simple <- c("EDR 1" = "#b3cde3", "EDR 2" = "#8c96c6", "EDR 3" = "#fe9929", "EDR 4" = "#8856a7", "EDR 5" = "#810f7c", "EDR 6E" = "#e5f5f9", "EDR 6W" = "#bdc9e1", "EDR 7E" = "#99d8c9", "EDR 7W" = "#2ca25f", "EDR 8" = "#74a9cf", "EDR 9" = "#0570b0", "EDR 10" = "#d7301f", "EDR 11" = "#d8b365", "Minnesota" = "black")

color.pr.edr <- c ("Northwest" = "#4575b4","Northeast" = "#e0f3f8", "Central" = "#fee090", "Seven County Mpls-St Paul" = "#d73027", "Southwest" = "#91bfdb", "Southeast" = "#fc8d59", "Minnesota" = "black", "EDR 1 - Northwest" = "#b3cde3", "EDR 2 - Headwaters" = "#8c96c6", "EDR 3 - Arrowhead" = "#fe9929", "EDR 4 - West Central" = "#8856a7", "EDR 5 - North Central" = "#810f7c", "EDR 6E- Southwest Central" = "#e5f5f9", "EDR 6W- Upper Minnesota Valley" = "#bdc9e1", "EDR 7E- East Central" = "#99d8c9", "EDR 7W- Central" = "#2ca25f", "EDR 8 - Southwest" = "#74a9cf", "EDR 9 - South Central" = "#0570b0", "EDR 10 - Southeast" = "#d7301f", "EDR 11 - 7 County Twin Cities" = "#d8b365")

mn_counties <- st_read("/Users/kellyasche/Library/CloudStorage/GoogleDrive-kasche@ruralmn.org/My Drive/Data Prep/R Projects/Shapefiles/County shapefiles/MNCounties_MNDOT.shp", quiet = TRUE) %>%
  ms_simplify(keep = .01, keep_shapes = TRUE) %>%
  rename(countyfp = FIPS_CODE)
```

```{r master}
master.original <- read_csv("Data/SLEDS/Masters/After analysis/Master-after-ct.csv") %>%
  mutate(ps.grad = ifelse(ps.grad == "Y", "Yes", ps.grad),
         highest.cred.level = ifelse(highest.cred.level %in% c("Associate degree", "Less than Associate Degree"), "Associate degree or lesser cred",
                                     ifelse(highest.cred.level %in% c("Some college", "No college"), "Did not grad ps", highest.cred.level)),
         ps.grad.InstitutionSector = ifelse(ps.grad.InstitutionSector == "1", "4-year public",
                                            ifelse(ps.grad.InstitutionSector == "4", "2-year public",
                                                   ifelse(ps.grad.InstitutionSector == "11", "Other type of college", ps.grad.InstitutionSector))),
         ps.grad.location = ifelse(ps.grad.location %in% c("PS grad EDR", "PS grad region"), "PS grad region", ps.grad.location))

kable(names(master.original))

kable(head(master.original))
```

# Dependent Variable

The cross-tab and ANOVA analysis exploring relationships between demographic, high school, and post-secondary variables with employment outcomes highlighted the importance of post-secondary variables in an individuals journey to meaningful employment inside and outside the region. Due to this, it makes sense to explore if some of the other variables play a role in post-secondary outcomes - whether an individual attends, graduates, the type of institution they attend and the location.

We will explore this here. We will explore this in three sections;

## Tier 1: Core Postsecondary Completion

These outcomes are the most foundational and are most closely tied to long-term employment and economic stability.

1.  **ps.grad** (graduated from postsecondary)

**Question:** Who completes a postsecondary credential?\
**Why it matters:** Credential completion is consistently the strongest predictor of sustained employment and earnings. This outcome captures whether students translate postsecondary participation into tangible labor market value.

2.  **highest.cred.level**

**Question:** What level of credential do graduates earn?\
**Why it matters:** Certificates, associate degrees, and bachelor’s degrees lead to very different employment opportunities and wage trajectories. This outcome distinguishes not just completion, but the depth of educational attainment.

## Tier 2: Secondary / Stratifying Postsecondary Outcomes

These outcomes add important context by differentiating *how* and *where* postsecondary education occurs.

3.  **ps.grad.InstitutionSector**

**Question:** From what type of institution do students graduate?\
**Why it matters:** Institutional sector (e.g., public two-year, public four-year, private) shapes cost, access, program offerings, and alignment with regional labor markets.

4.  **ps.grad.location** (used when geography matters)

**Question:** Where do students complete their postsecondary education?\
**Why it matters:** Location reveals patterns of mobility, regional retention, and place-based opportunity. It is especially relevant for understanding workforce pipelines and regional equity.

## Tier 3: Early Access Outcome (Optional)

This outcome captures initial engagement with postsecondary education and helps contextualize later attainment.

5.  **attended.ps** (ever attended postsecondary)

**Question:** Who participates in postsecondary education at any point?\
**Why it matters:** This provides a broad view of access beyond immediate enrollment, helping distinguish barriers to entry from barriers to completion.

Each dependent variable has the following categories;

```{r dependent variable categories}
data <- master.original %>%
  select(ps.grad, highest.cred.level, ps.grad.InstitutionSector, ps.grad.location, attended.ps) %>%
  mutate(across(c("ps.grad":"attended.ps"), factor))
```

**`ps.grad`**

```{r echo = FALSE}
levels(data$ps.grad)

```

**`highest.cred.level`**

```{r echo = FALSE}
levels(data$highest.cred.level)
```

`ps.grad.InstitutionSector`

```{r echo = FALSE}
levels(data$ps.grad.InstitutionSector)
```

`ps.grad.location`

```{r echo = FALSE}
levels(data$ps.grad.location)
```

`attended.ps`

```{r echo = FALSE}
levels(data$attended.ps)
```

<br>

# Predictors

For all of these, we will use the predictors that were in Tier 1 and Tier 2 of the previous cross-tab analysis.

-   Demographics
    -   `Gender`
    -   `economic.status`
    -   `RaceEthnicity`
    -   `SpecialEdStatus`
-   High school characteristics
    -   `Dem_Desc`
    -   `edr`
    -   `avg.unemp.rate`
    -   `avg.wage.pct.state`
-   High school accomplishments
    -   `ACTCompositeScore`
    -   `pseo.participant`
    -   `cte.achievement`
    -   `total.cte.courses.taken`
    -   `MCA.M`
    -   `MCA.R`
    -   `MCA.S`

<br>

# [**Tier 1: Core Postsecondary Completion**]{.underline}

Tier 1 examined how demographic background, high school context, and high school accomplishments predict three key milestones: (1) **immediate enrollment** (“attended.ps.within.first.year.hsgrad”), (2) **ever attending postsecondary** (“attended.ps”), and (3) **completing a credential** (“ps.grad”).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **Key Findings**

1.  **Demographics are the strongest and most consistent predictors across all three outcomes.**
    -   **Economic Status**: Economically disadvantaged students lagged behind at every stage—less likely to enroll immediately, less likely to attend at all, and much less likely to graduate.
    -   **Race/Ethnicity**: White and Asian/PI students consistently had higher enrollment and graduation rates, while American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students faced persistent barriers at all stages, with the largest disparities at completion.
    -   **Special Education Status**: Students with special education status showed markedly lower rates of enrollment, attendance, and especially graduation.
2.  **High School Characteristics showed weak associations.**
    -   **School Locale**: Rural, mixed, and urban settings had minimal differences in postsecondary pathways.
    -   **Community Economic Conditions**: Students from lower-unemployment, higher-wage areas were modestly more likely to enroll, attend, and graduate, but these effects were smaller than individual-level demographics or accomplishments.
3.  **High School Accomplishments were powerful predictors, especially for enrollment and graduation.**
    -   **Academic Achievement**: ACT and MCA scores (particularly Reading and Science) were strongly linked to both immediate enrollment and graduation, showing that academic readiness is a key driver of persistence and attainment.
    -   **PSEO Participation**: The single strongest programmatic predictor across all outcomes. PSEO students were much more likely to enroll immediately, attend at all, and eventually graduate.
    -   **CTE Involvement**: Higher CTE course-taking and concentrator/completor status were associated with lower likelihood of both enrollment and graduation, suggesting that intensive CTE pathways align more with direct workforce entry than with postsecondary progression.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## **Overall Conclusion**:

Tier 1 analysis reveals that **postsecondary access and attainment are shaped by both structural inequities and academic preparation**. Demographic barriers—economic disadvantage, special education status, and race/ethnicity—consistently depress outcomes at every stage. Academic readiness (ACT and MCA scores) and program participation (especially PSEO) are the most powerful positive predictors of successful transitions and completion. In contrast, CTE intensity is inversely associated with postsecondary outcomes, reflecting its alignment with alternative workforce pathways.

Together, these findings suggest that strategies to improve postsecondary success must focus on **closing equity gaps, bolstering academic readiness, and expanding access to PSEO and similar dual-enrollment programs**, while also balancing the role of CTE in supporting both workforce entry and potential academic progression.

<br>

## ps.grad

### Summary

\[Summary here\]

### Demographics

#### Ranked Demographic Variables by Strength of Relationship with Postsecondary Graduation (ps.grad)

**1. Race/Ethnicity (Cramér’s V = 0.298)**\
Race and ethnicity show the strongest relationship with postsecondary graduation. Completion rates vary widely across groups, with White students having the highest likelihood of graduating and substantially lower completion among American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students. American Indian students are particularly overrepresented among non-completers, indicating pronounced racial and ethnic inequities in postsecondary attainment.

**2. Economic Status (φ = 0.259)**\
Economic disadvantage is strongly associated with lower postsecondary graduation rates. Students eligible for free or reduced-price lunch are far less likely to complete a credential and are disproportionately represented among non-completers, highlighting persistent socioeconomic barriers to postsecondary success.

**3. Special Education Status (φ = 0.254)**\
Special education status is also strongly related to postsecondary graduation. Students who required special education services complete postsecondary credentials at much lower rates than their peers, suggesting substantial structural and support-related challenges in translating secondary education into postsecondary attainment.

**4. Gender (φ = 0.122)**\
Gender has the weakest relationship among the demographic variables examined, though it remains meaningful. Women are more likely than men to complete a postsecondary credential, while men are disproportionately represented among non-completers. Compared to other demographic factors, however, the gender gap is more modest.

::: panel-tabset
#### `Gender`

**Statistical relationship?:** Yes

**Strength of relationship:** Small-to-moderate (φ = 0.122, p \< .001)

**Description of relationship:** Women are more likely than men to complete a postsecondary credential. Just over half of women (50.3%) graduated from postsecondary education, compared with 38.1% of men. Men are disproportionately represented among non-completers, indicating a meaningful gender gap in postsecondary completion in Northwest Minnesota.

```{r}

data <- master.original %>%
  filter(ps.grad != "Attending ps")

tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad,
         var.col = data$Gender,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE)
```

#### `economic.status`

**Statistical relationship?:** Yes

**Strength of relationship:** Moderate (φ = 0.259, p \< .001)

**Description of relationship:** Economic status is strongly associated with postsecondary graduation. Individuals who were not eligible for free or reduced-price lunch are much more likely to complete a postsecondary credential (53.9%) than those who were eligible (27.3%). Conversely, nearly three-quarters of economically disadvantaged students do not complete postsecondary education, highlighting substantial and persistent socioeconomic barriers to college completion in Northwest Minnesota.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad,
         var.col = data$economic.status,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE)
```

#### `RaceEthnicity`

**Statistical relationship?:** Yes

**Strength of relationship:** Moderate-to-strong (Cramér’s V = 0.298, p \< .001)

**Description of relationship:** Postsecondary graduation rates vary substantially by race and ethnicity. White students have the highest completion rate (51.4%), while completion rates are notably lower for American Indian (16.2%), Black (24.4%), Hispanic (25.3%), and Asian/PI students (43.0%). American Indian students in particular are overwhelmingly represented among non-completers. These differences indicate pronounced racial and ethnic inequities in postsecondary completion in Northwest Minnesota, extending well beyond what would be expected by chance alone.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad,
         var.col = data$RaceEthnicity,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE)
```

#### `SpecialEdStatus` 

**Statistical relationship?:** Yes

**Strength of relationship:** Moderate (φ = 0.254, p \< .001)

**Description of relationship:** Special education status is strongly associated with postsecondary graduation. Individuals who did not receive special education services complete postsecondary credentials at much higher rates (49.4%) than those who did (14.8%). Students with special education needs are overwhelmingly represented among non-completers, indicating substantial structural and support-related barriers to postsecondary completion for this population in Northwest Minnesota.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad,
         var.col = data$SpecialEdStatus,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE)
```
:::

<br>

### High School Characteristics

**Key Findings**:

-   **School Locale (Dem_Desc)**: Cramer’s V = 0.022, p \< .001. Enrollment patterns were nearly identical across locales: rural (61.8%), town/rural mix (61.3%), and urban/town/rural mix (63.8%). The association is statistically significant but negligible in size.
-   **Average Unemployment Rate**: F(2, 49813) = 302.20, p \< .001. Students from higher-unemployment areas were less likely to enroll immediately, while immediate enrollees came from communities with relatively lower unemployment.
-   **Average Wages (as % of State Average)**: F(2, 49813) = 33.08, p \< .001. Students from higher-wage areas were somewhat more likely to attend postsecondary (either immediately or after a delay). Differences between immediate and delayed enrollment were not significant.

**Conclusion**:\
High school characteristics show weaker associations with immediate postsecondary enrollment than demographics. School locale has almost no impact, while local economic context matters somewhat—students in areas with higher unemployment and lower wages are less likely to transition into postsecondary. These results suggest that while community economic conditions shape opportunity, school setting itself does not strongly influence immediate enrollment.

<br>

::: panel-tabset
#### School Locale (Dem_Desc) → Attended Postsecondary Within First Year of High School Graduation

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 49.99, Cramer’s V = 0.022, p \< .001. Immediate enrollment (“Yes”) rates were similar across locales: entirely rural (61.8%), town/rural mix (61.3%), and urban/town/rural mix (63.8%). Non-enrollment (“Did not attend ps”) was slightly higher in town/rural mix schools (34.5%) compared to urban/town/rural mix (31.3%) and entirely rural (32.8%).

**Conclusion**:\
School locale shows a statistically significant but very weak association with immediate postsecondary enrollment. Differences across rural, mixed, and urban/town/rural contexts are small, suggesting that school setting itself is not a major driver of postsecondary transition.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master.original$attended.ps.within.first.year.hsgrad,
         var.col = master.original$Dem_Desc,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps-within-first-year-hsgrad/High school characteristics/Dem_Desc.html")
```

#### Average Unemployment Rate → Attended Postsecondary Within First Year of High School Graduation

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(2, 49813) = 302.20, p \< .001.\
Mean unemployment rates differed significantly across groups:

-   Did not attend postsecondary: highest average unemployment rate.
-   Delayed enrollment (“No”): about 0.68 percentage points lower.
-   Immediate enrollment (“Yes”): about 0.26 percentage points lower than those who never attended, and 0.41 points lower than those who delayed enrollment.

**Conclusion**:\
Students from areas with higher average unemployment rates are less likely to enroll in postsecondary immediately after high school. Immediate enrollees tend to come from communities with relatively lower unemployment, suggesting local labor market conditions may influence the timing of postsecondary transition.

```{r}
summary <- aov(avg.unemp.rate ~ attended.ps.within.first.year.hsgrad, data = master.original)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps-within-first-year-hsgrad/High school characteristics/avg-unemp-rate.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)


```

#### Average Wages (as % of Minnesota State Average) → Attended Postsecondary Within First Year of High School Graduation

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(2, 49813) = 33.08, p \< .001.

-   Students who never attended postsecondary were from areas with slightly lower average wages.
-   Immediate enrollees (“Yes”) came from areas with wages about 0.39 percentage points higher than those who never attended.
-   Delayed enrollees (“No”) also came from areas with slightly higher wages than non-attendees (0.45 points higher).
-   No significant difference was found between immediate and delayed enrollees.

**Conclusion**:\
Average local wages are modestly associated with postsecondary enrollment patterns. Students from communities with somewhat higher wages are more likely to pursue postsecondary education—whether immediately or after a delay—while students from lower-wage areas are more likely to never attend.

```{r}
summary <- aov(avg.wages.pct.state ~ attended.ps.within.first.year.hsgrad, data = master.original)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps-within-first-year-hsgrad/High school characteristics/avg-wage-pct-state.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```
:::

<br>

### High School Accomplishments

::: panel-tabset
#### ACT Composite Score → Attended Postsecondary Within First Year of High School Graduation

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(2, 28493) = 396.75, p \< .001.

-   Students who never attended postsecondary had the lowest ACT Composite Scores.
-   Delayed enrollees scored about 0.9 points higher on average than non-attendees.
-   Immediate enrollees scored about 2.0 points higher than non-attendees, and about 1.1 points higher than delayed enrollees.

**Conclusion**:\
ACT performance is strongly associated with immediate postsecondary enrollment. Higher ACT scores are linked to a greater likelihood of transitioning directly to postsecondary, while lower scores are tied to delaying or never attending.

```{r}
summary <- aov(ACTCompositeScore ~ attended.ps.within.first.year.hsgrad, data = master.original)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps-within-first-year-hsgrad/High school accomplishments/ACTCompositeScore.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### PSEO Participation → Attended Postsecondary Within First Year of High School Graduation

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 3454.84, Cramer’s V = 0.295, p \< .001. Immediate enrollment (“Yes”) was much higher among PSEO participants (76.8%) compared to non-participants (48.2%). Non-participants were more likely to never attend postsecondary (46.7% vs. 20.2%). Delayed enrollment was low for both groups, but slightly higher among non-participants (5.1% vs. 3.0%).

**Conclusion**:\
PSEO participation is strongly associated with immediate postsecondary enrollment. Students who participated in PSEO were far more likely to transition directly to postsecondary and much less likely to never attend, highlighting the program’s role in supporting successful transitions.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master.original$attended.ps.within.first.year.hsgrad,
         var.col = master.original$pseo.participant,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps-within-first-year-hsgrad/High school accomplishments/pseo-parcipant.html")
```

#### CTE Achievement → Attended Postsecondary Within First Year of High School Graduation

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 268.13, Cramer’s V = 0.052, p \< .001. Immediate enrollment (“Yes”) was highest among students with no CTE involvement (65.7%) and CTE participants (64.9%), while concentrators/completors had lower immediate enrollment (57.9%). Conversely, concentrators/completors were more likely to never attend postsecondary (37.1%) compared to participants (30.4%) or students with no CTE (29.6%).

**Conclusion**:\
CTE achievement shows a small but significant association with immediate postsecondary enrollment. Students deeply involved in CTE (concentrators/completors) are less likely to transition directly to postsecondary and more likely to never attend, while students with lighter or no CTE engagement transition at higher rates.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master.original$attended.ps.within.first.year.hsgrad,
         var.col = master.original$cte.achievement,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps-within-first-year-hsgrad/High school accomplishments/cte-achievement.html")
```

#### Total CTE Courses Taken → Attended Postsecondary Within First Year of High School Graduation

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(2, 49813) = 301.90, p \< .001.

-   Students who never attended postsecondary had taken the most CTE courses on average.
-   Delayed enrollees took about 0.25 fewer CTE courses than non-attendees.
-   Immediate enrollees took the fewest, averaging about 0.56 fewer courses than non-attendees and 0.31 fewer than delayed enrollees.

**Conclusion**:\
Greater CTE course-taking is associated with a lower likelihood of immediate postsecondary enrollment. Students who transition directly into postsecondary tend to have completed fewer CTE courses, while heavier CTE course loads are linked to delaying or forgoing postsecondary education.

```{r}
summary <- aov(total.cte.courses.taken ~ attended.ps.within.first.year.hsgrad, data = master.original)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps-within-first-year-hsgrad/High school accomplishments/total-cte-courses-taken.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### MCA Math Scores → Attended Postsecondary Within First Year of High School Graduation

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(2, 47621) = 885.58, p \< .001.

-   Students who never attended postsecondary had the lowest MCA Math scores.
-   Delayed enrollees scored slightly higher (0.12 points above non-attendees).
-   Immediate enrollees scored much higher—0.38 points above non-attendees and 0.50 points above delayed enrollees.

**Conclusion**:\
Math achievement is strongly associated with immediate postsecondary enrollment. Higher MCA Math scores are linked to a greater likelihood of enrolling right after high school, while lower scores are associated with delaying or never attending.

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.M ~ attended.ps.within.first.year.hsgrad, data = master.original)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps-within-first-year-hsgrad/High school accomplishments/MCA-M.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### MCA Reading Scores → Attended Postsecondary Within First Year of High School Graduation

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(2, 47319) = 1475.87, p \< .001.

-   Students who never attended postsecondary had the lowest MCA Reading scores.
-   Delayed enrollees scored about 0.15 points higher than non-attendees.
-   Immediate enrollees scored substantially higher—0.46 points above non-attendees and 0.31 points above delayed enrollees.

**Conclusion**:\
Reading achievement is strongly associated with immediate postsecondary enrollment. Higher MCA Reading scores are linked to a greater likelihood of transitioning directly into postsecondary, while lower scores are tied to delaying or never attending.

```{r}
  summary <- aov(MCA.R ~ attended.ps.within.first.year.hsgrad, data = master.original)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps-within-first-year-hsgrad/High school accomplishments/MCA-R.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### MCA Science Scores → Attended Postsecondary Within First Year of High School Graduation

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(2, 40378) = 1025.37, p \< .001.

-   Students who never attended postsecondary had the lowest MCA Science scores.
-   Delayed enrollees scored about the same as non-attendees (no significant difference).
-   Immediate enrollees scored much higher—0.41 points above non-attendees and 0.38 points above delayed enrollees.

**Conclusion**:\
Science achievement is strongly associated with immediate postsecondary enrollment. Higher MCA Science scores are linked to a greater likelihood of enrolling right after high school, while low scores are tied to non-enrollment or delay.

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.S ~ attended.ps.within.first.year.hsgrad, data = master.original)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps-within-first-year-hsgrad/High school accomplishments/MCA-S.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```
:::

<br>

## `Attended.ps`

### Summary

**Key Findings Across Variable Groups**

Demographics

-   **Economic Status**: φ = 0.228, p \< .001. Non-disadvantaged students were far more likely to attend (76%) than disadvantaged students (53.9%).
-   **Race/Ethnicity**: Cramer’s V = 0.164, p \< .001. White (72.4%) and Asian/PI (65.7%) students had the highest attendance, while American Indian students (51.8%) had the lowest, with Black (57.7%) and Hispanic (58.7%) also below average.
-   **Special Education Status**: φ = 0.273, p \< .001. Attendance was much higher for students without special education status (73.3%) compared to those with it (37.8%).

High School Characteristics

-   **School Locale (Dem_Desc)**: Cramer’s V = 0.029, p \< .001. Differences across rural, town/rural mix, and urban/town/rural mix were minimal (65–69%).
-   **Average Unemployment Rate**: F(1, 49814) = 428.40, p \< .001. Students from lower-unemployment areas were more likely to attend.
-   **Average Wages (% of State Average)**: F(1, 49814) = 65.84, p \< .001. Attendees came from areas with slightly higher average wages.

High School Accomplishments

-   **ACT Composite Score**: F(1, 28494) = 739.92, p \< .001. Attendees scored about 2 points higher than non-attendees.
-   **PSEO Participation**: φ = 0.279, p \< .001. Participation strongly boosted attendance (79.8% vs. 53.3%).
-   **CTE Achievement**: Cramer’s V = 0.071, p \< .001. Concentrators/completors had lower attendance (62.9%) than participants (69.6%) and students with no CTE (70.4%).
-   **Total CTE Courses Taken**: F(1, 49814) = 565.70, p \< .001. Non-attendees had taken more CTE courses.
-   **MCA Math**: F(1, 47622) = 1220.58, p \< .001. Attendees scored 0.35 points higher.
-   **MCA Reading**: F(1, 47320) = 2654.13, p \< .001. Attendees scored 0.44 points higher, the strongest MCA predictor.
-   **MCA Science**: F(1, 40379) = 1721.96, p \< .001. Attendees scored 0.39 points higher.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Overall Conclusion**:\
Postsecondary attendance is most strongly influenced by **demographic barriers** (economic disadvantage, special education status, and race/ethnicity), **program participation (PSEO)**, and **academic achievement (ACT and MCA scores, especially Reading)**. Community economic conditions (unemployment and wages) also play a role, though weaker, while **CTE involvement** shows an inverse relationship—students taking more CTE courses or reaching concentrator/completor status were less likely to attend.

Taken together, the results suggest that promoting equitable access to rigorous academics, reducing economic and special education barriers, and expanding opportunities like **PSEO** may be the most effective levers for improving overall postsecondary participation.

<br>

### Demographics

**Key Findings**:

-   **Economic Status**: φ = 0.228, p \< .001. Postsecondary attendance was higher for non-disadvantaged students (76%) than for economically disadvantaged students (53.9%). Disadvantaged students were almost twice as likely to never attend (46.1% vs. 24%).
-   **Race/Ethnicity**: Cramer’s V = 0.164, p \< .001. Attendance was highest for White (72.4%) and Asian/PI (65.7%) students. Attendance was lower among Hispanic (58.7%) and Black (57.7%) students, and lowest among American Indian students (51.8%). Students classified as “Unknown” also showed below-average rates (55.8%).
-   **Special Education Status**: φ = 0.273, p \< .001. Students without special education status were far more likely to attend postsecondary (73.3%) compared to students with special education status (37.8%). Special education students were more than twice as likely to never attend (62.2% vs. 26.7%).

**Conclusion**:\
Demographic factors are strongly associated with whether students ever attend postsecondary. Economic disadvantage and special education status present the largest barriers, while racial disparities are also significant, with American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students less likely to attend. These findings highlight that systemic inequities in access persist even when considering postsecondary attendance at any point after high school.

::: panel-tabset
#### Economic Status → Ever Attended Postsecondary (Attended.ps)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 2593.35, φ = 0.228, p \< .001. Postsecondary attendance was much higher among students not identified as economically disadvantaged (76%) compared to economically disadvantaged students (53.9%). Conversely, economically disadvantaged students were far more likely to never attend postsecondary (46.1% vs. 24%).

**Conclusion**:\
Economic status is strongly associated with whether a student ever attends postsecondary. Economically disadvantaged students are substantially less likely to attend, highlighting persistent inequities in long-term access to higher education.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master.original$attended.ps,
         var.col = master.original$economic.status,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps/Demographics/economic-status.html")
```

#### Race/Ethnicity → Ever Attended Postsecondary (Attended.ps)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 1345.33, Cramer’s V = 0.164, p \< .001. Postsecondary attendance was highest among White students (72.4%) and Asian/PI students (65.7%). Attendance was lower among Hispanic (58.7%) and Black students (57.7%), and lowest among American Indian students (51.8%). Students classified as “Unknown” were also below average (55.8%).

**Conclusion**:\
Race and ethnicity are significantly associated with whether students ever attend postsecondary. White and Asian/PI students are more likely to attend, while American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students are less likely to do so, highlighting persistent racial disparities in postsecondary access.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master.original$attended.ps,
         var.col = master.original$RaceEthnicity,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps/Demographics/RaceEthnicity.html")
```

#### Special Education Status → Ever Attended Postsecondary (Attended.ps)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 3707.63, φ = 0.273, p \< .001. Postsecondary attendance was much higher among students without special education status (73.3%) compared to students with special education status (37.8%). Conversely, special education students were much more likely to never attend postsecondary (62.2% vs. 26.7%).

**Conclusion**:\
Special education status is strongly associated with postsecondary attendance. Students with special education status are far less likely to ever attend, highlighting a major access gap that persists even when considering lifetime enrollment.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master.original$attended.ps,
         var.col = master.original$SpecialEdStatus,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps/Demographics/SpecialEdStatus.html")
```
:::

<br>

## High School Characteristics

**Key Findings**:

-   **School Locale (Dem_Desc)**: Cramer’s V = 0.029, p \< .001. Postsecondary attendance rates were nearly identical across locales: entirely rural (67.2%), town/rural mix (65.5%), and urban/town/rural mix (68.7%). Differences are negligible.
-   **Average Unemployment Rate**: F(1, 49814) = 428.40, p \< .001. Students from lower-unemployment areas were significantly more likely to attend postsecondary, with attendees coming from communities averaging 0.29 percentage points lower unemployment.
-   **Average Wages (as % of State Average)**: F(1, 49814) = 65.84, p \< .001. Postsecondary attendees came from areas with wages about 0.40 percentage points higher than those who never attended.

**Conclusion**:\
High school contextual factors show mixed influence on postsecondary attendance. School locale itself has virtually no impact, but broader community economic conditions matter: students from lower-unemployment, higher-wage areas are more likely to attend postsecondary. This suggests that local labor market opportunities and economic vitality influence long-term educational access more than school setting.

::: panel-tabset
#### School Locale (Dem_Desc) → Ever Attended Postsecondary (Attended.ps)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 42.93, Cramer’s V = 0.029, p \< .001. Postsecondary attendance rates were very similar across locales: entirely rural (67.2%), town/rural mix (65.5%), and urban/town/rural mix (68.7%).

**Conclusion**:\
School locale shows a statistically significant but negligible association with whether students ever attend postsecondary. Differences across rural, mixed, and urban/town/rural contexts are minimal, suggesting that school setting itself does not meaningfully influence overall postsecondary access.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master.original$attended.ps,
         var.col = master.original$Dem_Desc,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps/High school characteristics/Dem_Desc.html")
```

#### Average Unemployment Rate → Ever Attended Postsecondary (Attended.ps)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(1, 49814) = 428.40, p \< .001. Students who attended postsecondary came from areas with unemployment rates about 0.29 percentage points lower on average than those who never attended.

**Conclusion**:\
Community unemployment levels are significantly associated with postsecondary attendance. Students from higher-unemployment areas are less likely to ever attend postsecondary, suggesting that local labor market conditions play a role in shaping long-term educational access.

```{r}
summary <- aov(avg.unemp.rate ~ attended.ps, data = master.original)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps/High school characteristics/avg-unemp-rate.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)


```

#### Average Wages (as % of Minnesota State Average) → Ever Attended Postsecondary (Attended.ps)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(1, 49814) = 65.84, p \< .001. Students who attended postsecondary came from areas where average wages were about 0.40 percentage points higher than those who never attended.

**Conclusion**:\
Local wage levels show a modest but significant association with postsecondary attendance. Students from communities with higher relative wages are somewhat more likely to pursue postsecondary education, while those from lower-wage areas are less likely to attend.

```{r}
summary <- aov(avg.wages.pct.state ~ attended.ps, data = master.original)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps/High school characteristics/avg-wage-pct-state.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```
:::

<br>

## High School Accomplishments

**Key Findings**:

-   **ACT Composite Score**: F(1, 28494) = 739.92, p \< .001. Postsecondary attendees scored nearly 2 points higher on average than non-attendees.
-   **PSEO Participation**: φ = 0.279, p \< .001. Attendance was much higher among PSEO participants (79.8%) compared to non-participants (53.3%).
-   **CTE Achievement**: Cramer’s V = 0.071, p \< .001. Attendance was lowest among concentrators/completors (62.9%) compared to participants (69.6%) and students with no CTE (70.4%).
-   **Total CTE Courses Taken**: F(1, 49814) = 565.70, p \< .001. Non-attendees had taken more CTE courses, with attendees averaging 0.54 fewer courses.
-   **MCA Math**: F(1, 47622) = 1220.58, p \< .001. Attendees scored about 0.35 points higher than non-attendees.
-   **MCA Reading**: F(1, 47320) = 2654.13, p \< .001. Attendees scored about 0.44 points higher than non-attendees, the strongest MCA predictor.
-   **MCA Science**: F(1, 40379) = 1721.96, p \< .001. Attendees scored about 0.39 points higher than non-attendees.

**Conclusion**:\
High school accomplishments are strongly tied to postsecondary attendance. **PSEO participation** stands out as the most powerful programmatic predictor, while **academic achievement (ACT and MCA scores)** is consistently higher among attendees, especially reading scores. In contrast, heavier involvement in **CTE (more courses and concentrator/completor status)** is associated with lower attendance rates, suggesting a tradeoff between workforce-oriented preparation and pursuing postsecondary education.

::: panel-tabset
#### ACT Composite Score → Ever Attended Postsecondary (Attended.ps)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(1, 28494) = 739.92, p \< .001. Students who attended postsecondary had ACT Composite Scores nearly 2 points higher on average than those who never attended.

**Conclusion**:\
ACT performance is a strong predictor of postsecondary attendance. Higher ACT scores are strongly associated with a greater likelihood of attending postsecondary at some point after high school.

```{r}
summary <- aov(ACTCompositeScore ~ attended.ps, data = master.original)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps/High school accomplishments/ACTCompositeScore.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### PSEO Participation → Ever Attended Postsecondary (Attended.ps)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 3096.86, φ = 0.279, p \< .001. Postsecondary attendance was much higher among PSEO participants (79.8%) compared to non-participants (53.3%). Non-participants were far more likely to never attend postsecondary (46.7% vs. 20.2%).

**Conclusion**:\
PSEO participation is strongly associated with postsecondary attendance. Students who participated in PSEO were far more likely to attend postsecondary at some point, highlighting the program’s effectiveness in expanding long-term access to higher education.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master.original$attended.ps,
         var.col = master.original$pseo.participant,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps/High school accomplishments/pseo-participant.html")
```

#### CTE Achievement → Ever Attended Postsecondary (Attended.ps)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 254.60, Cramer’s V = 0.071, p \< .001. Postsecondary attendance was lowest among CTE concentrators/completors (62.9%) and higher among CTE participants (69.6%) and students with no CTE involvement (70.4%). Non-attendance was most common among concentrators/completors (37.1%) compared to participants (30.4%) and non-CTE students (29.6%).

**Conclusion**:\
CTE achievement shows a small but significant association with postsecondary attendance. Students deeply involved in CTE (concentrators/completors) are less likely to attend postsecondary compared to peers who were only participants or had no CTE courses.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = master.original$attended.ps,
         var.col = master.original$cte.achievement,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps/High school accomplishments/cte-achievement.html")
```

#### Total CTE Courses Taken → Ever Attended Postsecondary (Attended.ps)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(1, 49814) = 565.70, p \< .001. Students who attended postsecondary had taken fewer CTE courses on average, with a mean about 0.54 courses lower than those who never attended.

**Conclusion**:\
Greater CTE course-taking is negatively associated with postsecondary attendance. Students who took more CTE courses were less likely to pursue postsecondary education, suggesting that heavier CTE engagement may steer students toward workforce pathways rather than higher education.

```{r}
summary <- aov(total.cte.courses.taken ~ attended.ps, data = master.original)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps/High school accomplishments/total-cte-courses-taken.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### MCA Math Scores → Ever Attended Postsecondary (Attended.ps)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(1, 47622) = 1220.58, p \< .001. Students who attended postsecondary had MCA Math scores about 0.35 points higher on average than those who never attended.

**Conclusion**:\
Math achievement is strongly associated with postsecondary attendance. Higher MCA Math scores are linked to a greater likelihood of pursuing postsecondary education.

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.M ~ attended.ps, data = master.original)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps/High school accomplishments/MCA-M.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### MCA Reading Scores → Ever Attended Postsecondary (Attended.ps)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(1, 47320) = 2654.13, p \< .001. Students who attended postsecondary had MCA Reading scores about 0.44 points higher on average than those who never attended.

**Conclusion**:\
Reading achievement is very strongly associated with postsecondary attendance. Students with higher MCA Reading scores are substantially more likely to attend postsecondary at some point.

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.R ~ attended.ps, data = master.original)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps/High school accomplishments/MCA-R.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### MCA Science Scores → Ever Attended Postsecondary (Attended.ps)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(1, 40379) = 1721.96, p \< .001. Students who attended postsecondary had MCA Science scores about 0.39 points higher on average than those who never attended.

**Conclusion**:\
Science achievement is strongly associated with postsecondary attendance. Students with higher MCA Science scores are much more likely to attend postsecondary at some point compared to students with lower scores.

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.S ~ attended.ps, data = master.original)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/attended-ps/High school accomplishments/MCA-S.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```
:::

<br>

## `ps.grad`

### Summary

**Key Findings Across Variable Groups**

Demographics

-   **Economic Status**: φ = 0.270, p \< .001. Graduation was much higher among non-disadvantaged students (53.1%) compared to economically disadvantaged students (25.7%). Disadvantaged students were nearly three times as likely not to graduate.
-   **Race/Ethnicity**: Cramer’s V = 0.169, p \< .001 (Unknown removed). White (49.2%) and Asian/PI (44.7%) students had the highest graduation rates, while Hispanic (31.3%), Black (21.6%), and American Indian (20.8%) students had much lower rates.
-   **Special Education Status**: φ = 0.262, p \< .001. Students without special education status graduated at far higher rates (48.2%) than those with it (13.2%).

High School Characteristics

-   **School Locale (Dem_Desc)**: Cramer’s V = 0.022, p \< .001. Graduation rates were very similar across rural (39.2%), town/rural mix (41.6%), and urban/town/rural mix (43.2%).
-   **Average Unemployment Rate**: F(1, 44033) = 572.00, p \< .001. Graduates came from areas with unemployment rates about 0.33 percentage points lower than non-graduates.
-   **Average Wages (% of State Average)**: F(1, 44033) = 43.37, p \< .001. Graduates came from areas with wages about 0.33 percentage points higher than non-graduates.

High School Accomplishments

-   **ACT Composite Score**: F(1, 24476) = 1619.08, p \< .001. Graduates scored about 2.41 points higher than non-graduates.
-   **PSEO Participation**: φ = 0.308, p \< .001. Graduation was much more common among PSEO participants (57%) than non-participants (26.7%).
-   **CTE Achievement**: Cramer’s V = 0.103, p \< .001. Concentrators/completors had the lowest graduation rates (35.5%), while participants (42.7%) and non-CTE students (47.5%) graduated at higher rates.
-   **Total CTE Courses Taken**: F(1, 44033) = 693.05, p \< .001. Non-graduates had taken about 0.60 more CTE courses than graduates.
-   **MCA Math**: F(1, 41955) = 1748.50, p \< .001. Graduates scored 0.41 points higher than non-graduates.
-   **MCA Reading**: F(1, 41687) = 3087.39, p \< .001. Graduates scored 0.47 points higher, the strongest MCA predictor.
-   **MCA Science**: F(1, 34774) = 2188.12, p \< .001. Graduates scored 0.44 points higher.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Overall Conclusion**:\
Postsecondary graduation is shaped most strongly by **student-level factors**. Economic disadvantage and special education status are powerful barriers, while race and ethnicity reveal deep inequities in attainment. Among school-related experiences, **PSEO participation** stands out as the most influential positive predictor of graduation. Academic preparation (ACT and MCA scores, especially Reading and Science) also plays a major role. In contrast, **greater CTE involvement**—both in number of courses and concentrator/completor status—is linked to lower graduation rates, suggesting that heavy CTE pathways align more with workforce entry than degree attainment. Community economic conditions (lower unemployment and higher wages) show modest but consistent associations.

Taken together, these findings highlight that **supporting equity, strengthening academic readiness, and expanding dual-enrollment opportunities like PSEO** are critical levers for increasing postsecondary completion rates.

<br>

### Demographics

**Key Findings**:

-   **Economic Status**: φ = 0.270, p \< .001. Graduation was much more common among non-disadvantaged students (53.1%) compared to economically disadvantaged students (25.7%). Disadvantaged students were almost three times as likely not to graduate (74.3% vs. 46.9%).
-   **Race/Ethnicity**: Cramer’s V = 0.169, p \< .001 (Unknown removed). White (49.2%) and Asian/PI (44.7%) students had the highest graduation rates, while Hispanic (31.3%), Black (21.6%), and American Indian (20.8%) students graduated at much lower rates.
-   **Special Education Status**: φ = 0.262, p \< .001. Graduation rates were substantially higher for students without special education status (48.2%) compared to those with it (13.2%). Special education students were far more likely not to graduate (86.8% vs. 51.8%).

**Conclusion**:\
Demographic characteristics strongly influence postsecondary completion. Economic disadvantage and special education status are linked to particularly low graduation rates, while race and ethnicity reveal pronounced disparities, with White and Asian/PI students most likely to graduate and American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students least likely. These patterns indicate that structural barriers extend beyond access and deeply affect attainment.

::: panel-tabset
#### Economic Status → Postsecondary Graduation (Ps.grad)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 3204.41, φ = 0.270, p \< .001. Postsecondary graduation was much more common among students not economically disadvantaged (53.1%) compared to disadvantaged students (25.7%). Conversely, economically disadvantaged students were far more likely not to graduate (74.3% vs. 46.9%).

**Conclusion**:\
Economic status is strongly associated with postsecondary completion. Economically disadvantaged students are much less likely to graduate once they enroll, highlighting major inequities not only in access but also in degree attainment.

```{r}
data <- master.original %>%
  filter(ps.grad != "Attending ps")

tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad,
         var.col = data$economic.status,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad/Demographics/economic-status.html")
```

#### Race/Ethnicity → Postsecondary Graduation (Ps.grad) (Unknown Removed)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 1130.51, Cramer’s V = 0.169, p \< .001. Graduation rates varied by group:\
- White: 49.2% graduated, 50.8% did not.\
- Asian/PI: 44.7% graduated, 55.3% did not.\
- Hispanic: 31.3% graduated, 68.7% did not.\
- Black: 21.6% graduated, 78.4% did not.\
- American Indian: 20.8% graduated, 79.2% did not.

**Conclusion**:\
Race and ethnicity are significantly associated with postsecondary completion. White and Asian/PI students are the most likely to graduate, while Black, Hispanic, and American Indian students have substantially lower completion rates. This highlights persistent racial disparities in achieving postsecondary credentials.

```{r}
data <- master.original %>%
  filter(ps.grad != "Attending ps",
         RaceEthnicity != "Unknown")

tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad,
         var.col = data$RaceEthnicity,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad/Demographics/RaceEthnicity.html")
```

#### Special Education Status → Postsecondary Graduation (Ps.grad)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 3018.74, φ = 0.262, p \< .001. Postsecondary graduation was much higher among students without special education status (48.2%) compared to students with special education status (13.2%). Special education students were far more likely not to graduate (86.8% vs. 51.8%).

**Conclusion**:\
Special education status is strongly associated with postsecondary completion. Students with special education status are far less likely to graduate once they enroll, highlighting a significant attainment gap.

```{r}
data <- master.original %>%
  filter(ps.grad != "Attending ps")

tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad,
         var.col = data$SpecialEdStatus,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad/Demographics/SpecialEdStatus.html")
```
:::

<br>

### High School Characteristics

**Key Findings**:

-   **School Locale (Dem_Desc)**: Cramer’s V = 0.022, p \< .001. Graduation rates were similar across locales: entirely rural (39.2%), town/rural mix (41.6%), and urban/town/rural mix (43.2%). Differences are negligible.
-   **Average Unemployment Rate**: F(1, 44033) = 572.00, p \< .001. Graduates came from areas with unemployment rates about 0.33 percentage points lower than non-graduates.
-   **Average Wages (as % of State Average)**: F(1, 44033) = 43.37, p \< .001. Graduates came from areas with average wages about 0.33 percentage points higher than non-graduates.

**Conclusion**:\
High school contextual characteristics show limited but consistent influence on postsecondary graduation. Locale itself has virtually no impact, while broader community economic conditions—lower unemployment and higher wages—are modestly associated with greater likelihood of completing a credential. This suggests that local economic vitality provides some support for persistence through postsecondary education, though it is not a dominant factor compared to student-level characteristics.

::: panel-tabset
#### School Locale (Dem_Desc) → Postsecondary Graduation (Ps.grad)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 20.89, Cramer’s V = 0.022, p \< .001. Graduation rates were similar across locales: entirely rural (39.2%), town/rural mix (41.6%), and urban/town/rural mix (43.2%).

**Conclusion**:\
School locale has a statistically significant but negligible association with postsecondary graduation. Differences between rural, mixed, and urban/town/rural schools are very small, suggesting that locale has little impact on ultimate completion rates.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad,
         var.col = data$Dem_Desc,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad/High school characteristics/Dem_Desc.html")
```

#### Average Unemployment Rate → Postsecondary Graduation (Ps.grad)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(1, 44033) = 572.00, p \< .001. Students who graduated from postsecondary came from areas with unemployment rates about 0.33 percentage points lower than those who did not graduate.

**Conclusion**:\
Community unemployment levels are significantly associated with postsecondary completion. Students from lower-unemployment areas are more likely to graduate, suggesting that stronger local labor market conditions may support persistence and credential attainment.

```{r}
summary <- aov(avg.unemp.rate ~ ps.grad, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad/High school characteristics/avg-unemp-rate.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### Average Wages (as % of Minnesota State Average) → Postsecondary Graduation (Ps.grad)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(1, 44033) = 43.37, p \< .001. Students who graduated from postsecondary came from areas with average wages about 0.33 percentage points higher than those who did not graduate.

**Conclusion**:\
Local wage levels are modestly associated with postsecondary graduation. Students from somewhat higher-wage communities are more likely to complete a credential, suggesting that stronger local economic conditions may support persistence through postsecondary programs.

```{r}
summary <- aov(avg.wages.pct.state ~ ps.grad, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad/High school characteristics/avg-wage-pct-state.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```
:::

<br>

### High School Accomplishments

**Key Findings**:

-   **ACT Composite Score**: F(1, 24476) = 1619.08, p \< .001. Graduates scored about 2.41 points higher on average than non-graduates.
-   **PSEO Participation**: φ = 0.308, p \< .001. Graduation was much more common among PSEO participants (57%) than non-participants (26.7%).
-   **CTE Achievement**: Cramer’s V = 0.103, p \< .001. Graduation was lowest among CTE concentrators/completors (35.5%), compared to participants (42.7%) and students with no CTE involvement (47.5%).
-   **Total CTE Courses Taken**: F(1, 44033) = 693.05, p \< .001. Non-graduates had taken about 0.60 more CTE courses on average than graduates.
-   **MCA Math**: F(1, 41955) = 1748.50, p \< .001. Graduates scored 0.41 points higher than non-graduates.
-   **MCA Reading**: F(1, 41687) = 3087.39, p \< .001. Graduates scored 0.47 points higher than non-graduates, the strongest MCA predictor.
-   **MCA Science**: F(1, 34774) = 2188.12, p \< .001. Graduates scored 0.44 points higher than non-graduates.

**Conclusion**:\
High school accomplishments are strongly tied to postsecondary completion. **PSEO participation** is the single strongest programmatic predictor, while **academic achievement (ACT and MCA scores, particularly Reading and Science)** consistently predicts higher graduation rates. In contrast, **greater CTE engagement (more courses and concentrator/completor status)** is linked to lower graduation rates, suggesting a tradeoff between workforce preparation and degree attainment. Overall, academic preparation and dual-enrollment opportunities play critical roles in supporting successful postsecondary completion.

::: panel-tabset
#### ACT Composite Score → Postsecondary Graduation (Ps.grad)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(1, 24476) = 1619.08, p \< .001. Students who graduated from postsecondary had ACT Composite Scores about 2.41 points higher on average than those who did not graduate.

**Conclusion**:\
ACT performance is strongly associated with postsecondary completion. Higher ACT scores substantially increase the likelihood of graduating, underscoring the role of academic preparation in degree attainment.

```{r}
summary <- aov(ACTCompositeScore ~ ps.grad, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad/High school accomplishments/ACTCompositeScore.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### PSEO Participation → Postsecondary Graduation (Ps.grad)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 3210.71, φ = 0.308, p \< .001. Postsecondary graduation was much more common among PSEO participants (57%) compared to non-participants (26.7%). Non-participants were far more likely not to graduate (73.3% vs. 43%).

**Conclusion**:\
PSEO participation is strongly associated with postsecondary completion. Students who participated in PSEO were more than twice as likely to graduate, underscoring the program’s powerful role in promoting credential attainment.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad,
         var.col = data$pseo.participant,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad/High school accomplishments/pseo-participant.html")
```

#### CTE Achievement → Postsecondary Graduation (Ps.grad)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 465.86, Cramer’s V = 0.103, p \< .001. Graduation rates were lowest among CTE concentrators/completors (35.5%), higher among CTE participants (42.7%), and highest among students with no CTE involvement (47.5%). Non-graduation was most common among concentrators/completors (64.5%) compared to participants (57.3%) and non-CTE students (52.5%).

**Conclusion**:\
CTE achievement is significantly associated with postsecondary graduation. Students who were CTE concentrators or completors were less likely to graduate, while those with limited or no CTE participation had higher completion rates. This suggests that deeper CTE engagement may orient students more toward workforce pathways than degree attainment.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad,
         var.col = data$cte.achievement,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad/High school accomplishments/cte-achievement.html")
```

#### Total CTE Courses Taken → Postsecondary Graduation (Ps.grad)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(1, 44033) = 693.05, p \< .001. Students who graduated from postsecondary had taken about 0.60 fewer CTE courses on average compared to those who did not graduate.

**Conclusion**:\
Greater CTE course-taking is negatively associated with postsecondary completion. Students with heavier CTE course loads were less likely to graduate, reinforcing the pattern that strong engagement in CTE is linked to lower rates of credential attainment.

```{r}
summary <- aov(total.cte.courses.taken ~ ps.grad, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad/High school accomplishments/total-cte-courses-taken.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### MCA Math Scores → Postsecondary Graduation (Ps.grad)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(1, 41955) = 1748.50, p \< .001. Students who graduated from postsecondary had MCA Math scores about 0.41 points higher on average than those who did not graduate.

**Conclusion**:\
Math achievement is strongly associated with postsecondary completion. Higher MCA Math scores are linked to a greater likelihood of graduating, suggesting that math proficiency supports persistence and success through postsecondary programs.

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.M ~ ps.grad, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad/High school accomplishments/MCA-M.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### MCA Reading Scores → Postsecondary Graduation (Ps.grad)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(1, 41687) = 3087.39, p \< .001. Students who graduated from postsecondary had MCA Reading scores about 0.47 points higher on average than those who did not graduate.

**Conclusion**:\
Reading achievement is very strongly associated with postsecondary completion. Higher MCA Reading scores are linked to substantially greater likelihood of graduating, making reading proficiency one of the strongest academic predictors of credential attainment.

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.R ~ ps.grad, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad/High school accomplishments/MCA-R.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### MCA Science Scores → Postsecondary Graduation (Ps.grad)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(1, 34774) = 2188.12, p \< .001. Students who graduated from postsecondary had MCA Science scores about 0.44 points higher on average than those who did not graduate.

**Conclusion**:\
Science achievement is strongly associated with postsecondary completion. Higher MCA Science scores are linked to a significantly greater likelihood of graduating, reinforcing the importance of academic preparation in science for persistence and attainment.

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.S ~ ps.grad, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad/High school accomplishments/MCA-S.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```
:::

<br>

# [**Tier 2: Post-secondary stratification**]{.underline}

<br>

## `highest.cred.level`

### **Key Findings Across Variable Groups**

Demographics

-   **Economic Status**: Cramer’s V = 0.264, p \< .001. Non-disadvantaged students were far more likely to earn a bachelor’s or higher (46.2% vs. 20.4%), while disadvantaged students disproportionately stopped at “Some college” (49.2% vs. 26.6%).
-   **Race/Ethnicity**: Cramer’s V = 0.088, p \< .001. White (40.9%) and Asian/PI (52.5%) students had the highest bachelor’s+ attainment. Black (20.1%), Hispanic (26.7%), and American Indian (19.5%) students were far more likely to leave with “Some college” or sub-associate credentials.
-   **Special Education Status**: Cramer’s V = 0.233, p \< .001. Students with special education status were overwhelmingly concentrated in “Some college” (61.6%), with very few earning a bachelor’s+ (6.9%) compared to non-special education students (41.4%).

High School Characteristics

-   **School Locale (Dem_Desc)**: Cramer’s V = 0.028, p \< .001. Credential attainment was nearly identical across rural, mixed, and urban/town/rural schools.
-   **Average Unemployment Rate**: F(3, 28012) = 9.11, p \< .001. Students from higher-unemployment areas were slightly more likely to stop at sub-associate levels, while those from lower-unemployment areas were somewhat more likely to reach bachelor’s+.
-   **Average Wages (% of State Average)**: F(3, 28012) = 6.70, p \< .001. Students from higher-wage areas were modestly more likely to earn bachelor’s+, while those from lower-wage areas were slightly more likely to stop short.

High School Accomplishments

-   **ACT Composite Score**: F(3, 19493) = 1165.82, p \< .001. Higher ACT scores strongly predicted bachelor’s+ attainment. “Some college” students scored similarly to associate earners, indicating persistence rather than readiness drove non-completion.
-   **PSEO Participation**: Cramer’s V = 0.308, p \< .001. PSEO participants were more than twice as likely to earn a bachelor’s+ (50.5% vs. 22.2%) and much less likely to stop at “Some college.”
-   **CTE Achievement**: Cramer’s V = 0.140, p \< .001. CTE concentrators/completors were more likely to stop at associate degrees (28.2%) or “Some college” (38.9%) and less likely to reach bachelor’s+ (25.6%). Non-CTE students had the highest bachelor’s+ attainment (47%).
-   **Total CTE Courses Taken**: F(3, 28012) = 471.36, p \< .001. More CTE courses were associated with lower attainment; bachelor’s+ earners took the fewest CTE courses, while sub-associate and “Some college” groups took the most.
-   **MCA Math, Reading, Science**: All strongly associated with credential level (F \> 850, p \< .001). Bachelor’s+ earners consistently scored 0.3–0.6 points higher across subjects, with Reading and Science showing the strongest predictive value.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### **Overall Conclusion**

The level of postsecondary credential earned is shaped most powerfully by **demographic factors** and **academic accomplishments**. Economic disadvantage, special education status, and race/ethnicity all create steep barriers, concentrating students in sub-associate outcomes or “Some college” without completion. In contrast, strong academic preparation (ACT, MCA scores) and **PSEO participation** sharply increase the likelihood of earning a bachelor’s or higher degree. Intensive CTE engagement, while supporting workforce readiness, is consistently associated with lower attainment levels, especially associate degrees or non-completion.

**Community context (unemployment and wages)** exerts a modest effect, but school locale has virtually no impact. Overall, Tier 2 results highlight that **rigorous academics, equitable supports, and access to dual-enrollment opportunities** are critical levers for ensuring students not only graduate but also achieve higher levels of credential attainment.

<br>

### Demographics

**Key Findings**:

-   **Economic Status**: Cramer’s V = 0.264, p \< .001. Non-disadvantaged students were far more likely to complete a bachelor’s or higher degree (46.2% vs. 20.4%) while disadvantaged students disproportionately left with “Some college” and no degree (49.2% vs. 26.6%).
-   **Race/Ethnicity**: Cramer’s V = 0.088, p \< .001. White (40.9%) and Asian/PI (52.5%) students were most likely to earn bachelor’s or higher degrees, while American Indian (19.5%), Black (20.1%), and Hispanic (26.7%) students disproportionately stopped short, often leaving with no degree.
-   **Special Education Status**: Cramer’s V = 0.233, p \< .001. Students with special education status were overwhelmingly concentrated in “Some college” (61.6%), with only 6.9% earning a bachelor’s or higher, compared to 41.4% of non-special education students.

**Conclusion**:\
Demographic factors play a major role in shaping the highest credential attained. **Economic disadvantage** and **special education status** are the strongest barriers, sharply reducing the likelihood of earning a bachelor’s or higher degree. **Racial disparities** also persist, with White and Asian/PI students attaining higher levels of education, while American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students are much more likely to leave postsecondary without completing a credential. These patterns highlight that inequities extend well beyond access and graduation, influencing the ultimate level of educational attainment students are able to achieve.

::: panel-tabset
#### Economic Status → Highest Credential Level (Highest.cred.level)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 1959.32, Cramer’s V = 0.264, p \< .001.
