-   **Non-disadvantaged students** were much more likely to earn a bachelor’s degree or higher (46.2%) compared to disadvantaged students (20.4%).
-   **Disadvantaged students** were far more likely to leave with “Some college” and no degree (49.2% vs. 26.6%).
-   Associate’s and sub-associate credentials showed smaller gaps, with disadvantaged students slightly more likely to stop at these levels.

**Conclusion**:\
Economic status is strongly associated with the highest credential earned. Economically disadvantaged students are far less likely to reach a bachelor’s degree or higher and far more likely to leave postsecondary without completing a credential. This points to economic status as a key driver not only of access but also of persistence and attainment level.

```{r}
data <- master.original %>%
  filter(!highest.cred.level %in% c("Attending ps", "No college"))

tab_xtab(var.row = data$highest.cred.level,
         var.col = data$economic.status,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/highest-cred-level/Demographics/economic-status.html")
```

#### Race/Ethnicity → Highest Credential Level (Highest.cred.level)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 627.58, Cramer’s V = 0.088, p \< .001. Patterns of attainment varied:

-   **White students** were the most likely to earn a bachelor’s or higher (40.9%) and less likely to stop at “Some college” (31.2%).
-   **Asian/PI students** also had high bachelor’s/graduate attainment (52.5%), with relatively fewer leaving with no degree (30.2%).
-   **Black students** were the least likely to reach a bachelor’s or higher (20.1%) and most likely to stop at “Some college” (62.1%).
-   **Hispanic students** had lower bachelor’s/graduate attainment (26.7%) and higher “Some college” outcomes (45.4%).
-   **American Indian students** showed a similar pattern, with only 19.5% completing bachelor’s or higher and more than half (55.2%) leaving with no degree.

**Conclusion**:\
Race and ethnicity are significantly associated with the highest credential earned, though the effect size is modest. White and Asian/PI students are far more likely to achieve bachelor’s or higher degrees, while American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students are disproportionately likely to leave postsecondary without completing a degree. This reflects persistent racial disparities not only in access and graduation, but also in the ultimate level of attainment.

```{r}
data <- master.original %>%
  filter(!highest.cred.level %in% c("Attending ps", "No college"),
         RaceEthnicity != "Unknown")

tab_xtab(var.row = data$highest.cred.level,
         var.col = data$RaceEthnicity,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/highest-cred-level/Demographics/RaceEthnicity.html")
```

#### Special Education Status → Highest Credential Level (Highest.cred.level)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 1522.15, Cramer’s V = 0.233, p \< .001.

-   **Students without special education status**: 41.4% earned a bachelor’s degree or higher, 30.7% left with “Some college,” 16.2% stopped at an associate’s, and 11.7% earned less than an associate credential.
-   **Students with special education status**: Only 6.9% earned a bachelor’s or higher. The vast majority either left with “Some college” and no degree (61.6%) or stopped at an associate’s degree (10.8%).

**Conclusion**:\
Special education status is strongly associated with the highest credential attained. Students with special education status are overwhelmingly concentrated in the “Some college” category, with very limited progression to bachelor’s or higher degrees. This reflects persistent barriers to full attainment among students with special education needs.

```{r}
data <- master.original %>%
  filter(!highest.cred.level %in% c("Attending ps", "No college"))

tab_xtab(var.row = data$highest.cred.level,
         var.col = data$SpecialEdStatus,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/highest-cred-level/Demographics/SpecialEdStatus.html")
```
:::

<br>

## High School Characteristics

**Key Findings**:

-   **School Locale (Dem_Desc)**: Cramer’s V = 0.028, p \< .001. Credential attainment was nearly identical across rural, mixed, and urban/town/rural schools, with differences of only a few percentage points.
-   **Average Unemployment Rate**: F(3, 28012) = 9.11, p \< .001. Students from higher-unemployment areas were modestly more likely to stop at sub-associate credentials, while those from lower-unemployment areas were somewhat more likely to reach higher credentials.
-   **Average Wages (as % of State Average)**: F(3, 28012) = 6.70, p \< .001. Students from higher-wage areas were slightly more likely to earn bachelor’s or higher degrees, while students from lower-wage areas were somewhat more likely to stop short of an associate degree.

**Conclusion**:\
High school contextual characteristics have only limited influence on the highest credential earned. **School locale shows virtually no effect**, while **local economic conditions**—lower unemployment and higher wages—are modestly associated with higher attainment levels. These community effects are real but small, and far less impactful than demographic or academic factors.

::: panel-tabset
#### School Locale (Dem_Desc) → Highest Credential Level (Highest.cred.level)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 45.47, Cramer’s V = 0.028, p \< .001. Credential attainment patterns were very similar across locales:

-   **Entirely rural**: 38.5% stopped at “Some college,” 32.9% earned a bachelor’s or higher.
-   **Town/rural mix**: 32.8% “Some college,” 37.7% bachelor’s or higher.
-   **Urban/town/rural mix**: 33.2% “Some college,” 39.1% bachelor’s or higher.

Differences across locales were minimal, generally within 5 percentage points.

**Conclusion**:\
School locale has a statistically significant but negligible association with the highest credential earned. Students from rural, mixed, and urban/town/rural schools attain similar levels of postsecondary credentials, suggesting locale plays little role in shaping ultimate attainment.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data$highest.cred.level,
         var.col = data$Dem_Desc,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/highest-cred-level/High school characteristics/Dem_Desc.html")
```

#### Average Unemployment Rate → Highest Credential Level (Highest.cred.level)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(3, 28012) = 9.11, p \< .001.

-   Students whose highest attainment was **less than an associate degree** came from areas with slightly higher unemployment rates compared to both associate and bachelor’s+ earners (difference ≈ 0.11 percentage points, p \< .01).
-   No significant differences emerged between bachelor’s and associate degree earners, or between bachelor’s and “Some college.”

**Conclusion**:\
Community unemployment levels show a small but significant association with the highest credential attained. Students from higher-unemployment areas are modestly more likely to stop at sub-associate credentials, while those from lower-unemployment areas are somewhat more likely to advance to higher levels. Overall, the effect is statistically real but substantively limited.

```{r}
summary <- aov(avg.unemp.rate ~ highest.cred.level, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/highest-cred-level/High school characteristics/avg.unemp.rate.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### Average Wages (as % of Minnesota State Average) → Highest Credential Level (Highest.cred.level)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(3, 28012) = 6.70, p \< .001.

-   Students who attained a **bachelor’s degree or higher** came from areas with slightly higher relative wages than associate earners (difference ≈ 0.42 percentage points, p \< .001).
-   Students with **less than an associate degree** also came from somewhat lower-wage areas than associate earners (difference ≈ 0.38 percentage points, p \< .05).
-   No significant differences emerged between “Some college” and either associate or bachelor’s groups.

**Conclusion**:\
Local wage levels show a modest association with the highest credential earned. Students from higher-wage areas are somewhat more likely to complete bachelor’s or higher degrees, while those from lower-wage areas are slightly more likely to stop short of an associate degree. These effects are real but small in magnitude compared to student-level demographic and academic factors.

```{r}
summary <- aov(avg.wages.pct.state ~ highest.cred.level, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/highest-cred-level/High school characteristics/avg.wage.pct.state.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```
:::

<br>

## High School Accomplishments

**Key Findings**:

-   **ACT Composite Score**: F(3, 19493) = 1165.82, p \< .001. Students earning a bachelor’s or higher scored 3–4 points above those stopping at associate or sub-associate levels. “Some college” students had ACT scores similar to associate earners, suggesting non-completion is tied more to persistence than readiness.
-   **PSEO Participation**: Cramer’s V = 0.308, p \< .001. PSEO participants were more than twice as likely to earn a bachelor’s or higher (50.5% vs. 22.2%) and far less likely to leave with “Some college” (23.9% vs. 44.9%). Associate and sub-associate outcomes were nearly identical between groups.
-   **CTE Achievement**: Cramer’s V = 0.140, p \< .001. Concentrators/completors were less likely to earn bachelor’s+ (25.6%) and more likely to stop at associate (28.2%) or “Some college” (38.9%). Non-CTE students had the highest bachelor’s+ attainment (47%).
-   **Total CTE Courses Taken**: F(3, 28012) = 471.36, p \< .001. Students who earned bachelor’s+ took fewer CTE courses, while those with sub-associate or “Some college” outcomes had taken more. Largest gap: bachelor’s+ vs. sub-associate, with \>1.3 course difference.
-   **MCA Math**: F(3, 27568) = 925.88, p \< .001. Bachelor’s+ earners scored 0.45–0.66 points higher than other groups. Lower scores characterized sub-associate and “Some college” outcomes.
-   **MCA Reading**: F(3, 27410) = 1053.96, p \< .001. Bachelor’s+ earners scored 0.32–0.59 points higher than other groups, making reading one of the strongest predictors of higher attainment.
-   **MCA Science**: F(3, 22192) = 854.01, p \< .001. Bachelor’s+ earners scored 0.41–0.60 points higher than other groups, while “Some college” and sub-associate students scored notably lower.

**Conclusion**:\
High school accomplishments are among the most powerful predictors of the highest credential earned. **PSEO participation** stands out as the strongest programmatic factor, dramatically increasing the likelihood of bachelor’s or higher attainment. **Academic performance (ACT, MCA Reading, and MCA Science especially)** is tightly linked to ultimate attainment, differentiating bachelor’s+ earners from those who stop earlier. In contrast, **greater CTE engagement** (more courses and concentrator/completor status) is associated with lower levels of attainment, with students more likely to finish at the associate level or leave with “Some college.” Overall, rigorous academics and early postsecondary experiences strongly support higher credential attainment, while intensive CTE pathways align more with sub-baccalaureate outcomes.

::: panel-tabset
#### ACT Composite Score → Highest Credential Level (Highest.cred.level)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(3, 19493) = 1165.82, p \< .001.

-   Students who earned a **bachelor’s degree or higher** had ACT scores about 3.39 points higher than associate degree earners, and about 4.22 points higher than those who stopped short of an associate degree.
-   Students with **less than an associate degree** had significantly lower ACT scores than associate earners (–0.83).
-   Students in the **“Some college”** group scored similarly to associate earners (no significant difference).

**Conclusion**:\
ACT performance is strongly associated with the highest credential attained. Higher ACT scores are linked to completing a bachelor’s or higher degree, while lower scores increase the likelihood of stopping short of an associate degree. Students who attended college without finishing a credential had ACT scores similar to associate earners, suggesting that non-completion is driven more by persistence factors than academic readiness alone.

```{r}
summary <- aov(ACTCompositeScore ~ highest.cred.level, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/highest-cred-level/High school accomplishments/ACTCompositeScore.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### PSEO Participation → Highest Credential Level (Highest.cred.level)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 1933.77, Cramer’s V = 0.308, p \< .001.

-   **PSEO participants** were far more likely to complete a bachelor’s degree or higher (50.5%) compared to non-participants (22.2%).
-   Participants were also less likely to leave with “Some college” and no degree (23.9% vs. 44.9%).
-   Rates of associate degree and sub-associate credential attainment were nearly identical between participants and non-participants (\~16%).

**Conclusion**:\
PSEO participation is strongly associated with higher credential attainment. Students who participated in PSEO were more than twice as likely to earn a bachelor’s or higher degree and far less likely to leave postsecondary without a credential. This highlights PSEO as one of the most effective levers for boosting ultimate educational attainment.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data$highest.cred.level,
         var.col = data$pseo.participant,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/highest-cred-level/High school accomplishments/pseo-participant.html")
```

#### CTE Achievement → Highest Credential Level (Highest.cred.level)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 1097.17, Cramer’s V = 0.140, p \< .001. Credential attainment patterns differed by CTE involvement:

-   **CTE concentrators/completors** were the least likely to earn a bachelor’s or higher (25.6%) and the most likely to leave with “Some college” (38.9%). They were also more likely than other groups to stop at the associate degree level (28.2%).
-   **CTE participants** showed more balanced outcomes, with 36.9% earning a bachelor’s or higher, 34% stopping at “Some college,” and 20.3% earning an associate’s degree.
-   **Non-CTE students** were the most likely to reach a bachelor’s or higher (47%) and the least likely to stop at associate’s (15.7%) or “Some college” (29.8%).

**Conclusion**:\
CTE achievement is significantly associated with the highest credential attained. Students with deeper CTE engagement (concentrators/completors) are less likely to earn a bachelor’s or higher degree and more likely to stop at an associate degree or leave with “Some college.” In contrast, students with no CTE experience have the highest rates of bachelor’s and graduate-level attainment. This highlights how intensive CTE pathways tend to align with sub-baccalaureate outcomes and workforce preparation rather than advanced academic credentials.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data$highest.cred.level,
         var.col = data$cte.achievement,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/highest-cred-level/High school accomplishments/cte-achievement.html")
```

#### Total CTE Courses Taken → Highest Credential Level (Highest.cred.level)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(3, 28012) = 471.36, p \< .001.

-   Students who earned a **bachelor’s degree or higher** had taken fewer CTE courses on average than associate degree earners (–0.72 courses, p \< .001).
-   Students with **less than an associate degree** had taken significantly more CTE courses than associate earners (+0.63, p \< .001).
-   Students who left with **“Some college”** had also taken slightly more CTE courses than associate earners (+0.13, p \< .01).
-   The largest gap was between **bachelor’s+ earners and those with less than an associate degree**, with the latter taking over 1.35 more CTE courses on average.

**Conclusion**:\
Greater CTE course-taking is negatively associated with ultimate attainment. Students who take more CTE courses are less likely to earn a bachelor’s or higher degree and more likely to stop short—either with “Some college” or with credentials below the associate level. In contrast, students who take fewer CTE courses are disproportionately represented among bachelor’s and graduate degree earners, reinforcing the strong alignment of intensive CTE pathways with sub-baccalaureate outcomes.

```{r}
summary <- aov(total.cte.courses.taken ~ highest.cred.level, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/highest-cred-level/High school accomplishments/total-cte-courses-taken.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### MCA Math Scores → Highest Credential Level (Highest.cred.level)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(3, 27568) = 925.88, p \< .001.

-   Students with a **bachelor’s degree or higher** scored about 0.45 points higher than associate degree earners, and about 0.66 points higher than those with less than an associate degree.
-   Students in the **“Some college”** group scored 0.20 points lower than associate earners, and about 0.66 points lower than bachelor’s+ earners.
-   Students with **less than an associate degree** scored 0.21 points lower than associate earners.

**Conclusion**:\
Math achievement is strongly associated with the highest credential earned. Students with higher MCA Math scores are far more likely to complete bachelor’s or higher degrees, while those with lower scores are more likely to stop at associate’s, sub-associate credentials, or leave with “Some college.”

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.M ~ highest.cred.level, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/highest-cred-level/High school accomplishments/MCA-M.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### MCA Reading Scores → Highest Credential Level (Highest.cred.level)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(3, 27410) = 1053.96, p \< .001.

-   Students with a **bachelor’s degree or higher** scored about 0.32 points higher than associate degree earners, 0.59 points higher than those with less than an associate degree, and 0.54 points higher than those who left with “Some college.”
-   Students with **less than an associate degree** and those with **“Some college”** scored significantly lower than both associate earners and bachelor’s+ earners.

**Conclusion**:\
Reading achievement is very strongly associated with the highest credential earned. Higher MCA Reading scores sharply increase the likelihood of attaining a bachelor’s or higher degree, while lower scores are linked to stopping at sub-associate levels or leaving with no degree. This makes reading proficiency one of the clearest academic differentiators of ultimate educational attainment.

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.R ~ highest.cred.level, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/highest-cred-level/High school accomplishments/MCA-R.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### MCA Science Scores → Highest Credential Level (Highest.cred.level)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(3, 22192) = 854.01, p \< .001.

-   Students with a **bachelor’s degree or higher** scored about 0.41 points higher than associate earners, 0.57 points higher than sub-associate earners, and 0.60 points higher than those with “Some college.”
-   Students with **less than an associate degree** scored about 0.16 points lower than associate earners.
-   Students with **“Some college”** scored about 0.18 points lower than associate earners.

**Conclusion**:\
Science achievement is strongly associated with the highest credential earned. Students with higher MCA Science scores are far more likely to attain bachelor’s or higher degrees, while those with lower scores are concentrated among associate earners, sub-associate credentials, or incomplete “Some college” outcomes.

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.S ~ highest.cred.level, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/highest-cred-level/High school accomplishments/MCA-S.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```
:::

<br>

# [**Tier 3: Post-secondary access**]{.underline}

## ps.grad.location

Tier 3 examined how demographic factors, high school characteristics, and high school accomplishments shape where students complete their postsecondary credentials: **within their high school region, elsewhere in Minnesota, or outside the state.**

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### **Key Findings**

Demographics

-   **Economic Status**: Economically disadvantaged students were more likely to graduate within their home region (67.8% vs. 55.7%), while non-disadvantaged students showed greater mobility, more often graduating elsewhere in MN or out of state.
-   **Race/Ethnicity**: American Indian (67.8%) and Hispanic (63.6%) graduates were most likely to stay in-region, while Asian/PI graduates were the least likely (48.7%), with higher rates graduating elsewhere in MN or out of state. White graduates showed a balanced pattern.
-   **Special Education Status**: Students with special education status were overwhelmingly more likely to remain in-region (73.8%) than non-special education students (57.7%), who were more mobile across the state and beyond.

High School Characteristics

-   **School Locale**: Rural graduates were less likely to remain in-region (38%) and more likely to graduate elsewhere in MN, while mixed/urban graduates were more likely to stay local (56–61%).
-   **Economic Conditions**: Students from lower-unemployment, higher-wage areas were more likely to graduate in their home region or outside the state, while those from higher-unemployment, lower-wage areas were more likely to complete elsewhere in Minnesota. Effects were modest but consistent.

High School Accomplishments

-   **Academic Achievement (ACT & MCA scores)**: Students with higher ACT and MCA scores were significantly more likely to graduate elsewhere in Minnesota or out of state. Lower-scoring students were more likely to remain in-region.
-   **PSEO Participation**: Participants were more geographically mobile, with higher rates of completing elsewhere in Minnesota or outside the state, while non-participants were more likely to stay local.
-   **CTE Engagement**: Students with deeper CTE involvement (more courses, concentrator/completor status) were far more likely to graduate in-region, while students with little or no CTE experience showed greater mobility.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

### **Overall Conclusion**

Tier 3 results show that **geographic mobility in postsecondary completion is closely tied to academic strength, program participation, and demographics.**

-   Students who are **academically stronger or PSEO participants** are more likely to graduate away from their home region, either elsewhere in Minnesota or out of state.
-   Students who are **economically disadvantaged, American Indian, Hispanic, or in special education**, as well as those with deeper **CTE engagement**, are more likely to remain local.
-   **Community economic conditions** and **school locale** play smaller but notable roles, with rural and lower-wage contexts reinforcing regional completion.

In sum, Tier 3 highlights a divide between **mobile, academically prepared students** who pursue credentials beyond their home region, and **students facing structural or programmatic constraints** who are more likely to complete locally. This underscores the importance of academic preparation, resource access, and program opportunities in shaping not just whether students succeed in postsecondary, but **where** they do so.

<br>

### Demographics

**Key Findings**:

-   **Economic Status**: Cramer’s V = 0.104, p \< .001. Economically disadvantaged students were more likely to graduate within their high school region (67.8% vs. 55.7%), while non-disadvantaged students were more likely to graduate elsewhere in Minnesota (25.8% vs. 19.9%) or outside the state (18.5% vs. 12.3%).
-   **Race/Ethnicity**: Cramer’s V = 0.051, p \< .001. American Indian (67.8%) and Hispanic (63.6%) graduates were most likely to remain in-region, while Asian/PI graduates were the least likely (48.7%), with higher rates completing elsewhere in MN (30.5%) or outside MN (20.8%). White graduates showed balanced outcomes across regions.
-   **Special Education Status**: Cramer’s V = 0.072, p \< .001. Students with special education status were far more likely to remain in their high school region (73.8% vs. 57.7%), while non-special education students were more likely to complete elsewhere in MN (24.9% vs. 14.8%) or outside the state (17.4% vs. 11.4%).

**Conclusion**:\
Demographic characteristics modestly influence the location of postsecondary graduation. **Economically disadvantaged, American Indian, Hispanic, and special education students are more likely to stay within their home region**, reflecting limited geographic mobility. In contrast, **non-disadvantaged, White, and Asian/PI students show greater mobility**, more often completing their credentials elsewhere in Minnesota or out of state. These differences suggest that resources, community ties, and support needs shape students’ ability or decision to pursue postsecondary opportunities beyond their home region.

::: panel-tabset
#### Economic Status → Postsecondary Graduation Location (Ps.grad.location)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 202.79, Cramer’s V = 0.104, p \< .001.

-   **Economically disadvantaged students** were more likely to graduate within their high school region (67.8%) compared to non-disadvantaged peers (55.7%).
-   **Non-disadvantaged students** were more likely to graduate elsewhere in Minnesota (25.8% vs. 19.9%) or outside the state (18.5% vs. 12.3%).

**Conclusion**:\
Economic status is modestly associated with where students complete postsecondary credentials. Economically disadvantaged students tend to stay closer to home, graduating within their high school region, while non-disadvantaged students are more geographically mobile, more often completing credentials outside their region, both within and outside Minnesota.

```{r}
data <- master.original %>%
  filter(!ps.grad.location %in% c("Attending ps", "Did not graduate ps"))

tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad.location,
         var.col = data$economic.status,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad-location/Demographics/economic-status.html")
```

#### Race/Ethnicity → Postsecondary Graduation Location (Ps.grad.location)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 98.74, Cramer’s V = 0.051, p \< .001. Location of graduation showed modest differences by race/ethnicity:

-   **American Indian (67.8%)** and **Hispanic (63.6%)** graduates were most likely to remain in their high school region.
-   **Black graduates (57.0%)** also tended to remain in-region, but with a somewhat higher share completing outside MN (20.1%).
-   **White graduates (57.9%)** were more balanced, with 24.8% completing elsewhere in Minnesota and 17.3% outside the state.
-   **Asian/PI graduates (48.7%)** were the least likely to remain in-region, with higher rates of completing elsewhere in Minnesota (30.5%) or outside MN (20.8%).

**Conclusion**:\
Race and ethnicity are significantly associated with where students graduate from postsecondary institutions, though the effect size is small. American Indian and Hispanic graduates are more likely to remain in-region, while Asian/PI and White graduates show greater mobility, often completing outside their region or out of state. These patterns suggest varying levels of geographic mobility across racial groups, which may reflect differences in access, resources, or cultural and community ties.

```{r}

tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad.location,
         var.col = data$RaceEthnicity,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad-location/Demographics/RaceEthnicity.html")
```

#### Special Education Status → Postsecondary Graduation Location (Ps.grad.location)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 95.81, Cramer’s V = 0.072, p \< .001.

-   **Students with special education status** were much more likely to graduate within their high school region (73.8%) compared to non-special education students (57.7%).
-   **Non-special education students** were more likely to graduate elsewhere in Minnesota (24.9% vs. 14.8%) or outside the state (17.4% vs. 11.4%).

**Conclusion**:\
Special education status is modestly associated with where students complete postsecondary credentials. Students with special education needs are more likely to stay local and graduate within their high school region, while non-special education students demonstrate greater geographic mobility, completing degrees elsewhere in Minnesota or out of state.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad.location,
         var.col = data$SpecialEdStatus,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad-location/Demographics/SpecialEdStatus.html")
```
:::

<br>

## High School Characteristics

**Key Findings**:

-   **School Locale (Dem_Desc)**: Cramer’s V = 0.113, p \< .001. Graduates from entirely rural schools were less likely to remain in-region (38%) and more likely to graduate elsewhere in Minnesota (49.3%). Graduates from town/rural mix (55.9%) and urban/town/rural mix schools (61%) were more likely to stay in-region.
-   **Average Unemployment Rate**: F(2, 18642) = 19.17, p \< .001. Graduates from lower-unemployment areas were slightly more likely to graduate in their home region or outside Minnesota, while graduates from higher-unemployment areas were more likely to graduate elsewhere in Minnesota.
-   **Average Wages (as % of State Average)**: F(2, 18642) = 107.57, p \< .001. Students from higher-wage areas were more likely to graduate either in their home region or outside the state, while those from lower-wage areas were more likely to graduate elsewhere in Minnesota.

**Conclusion**:\
High school contextual characteristics exert a modest influence on where students complete postsecondary credentials. **Locale matters somewhat**, with rural students more likely to leave their region for other Minnesota institutions, while mixed/urban students are more likely to remain local. **Economic conditions also play a role**: students from stronger labor markets (lower unemployment, higher wages) are more likely to complete either locally or out of state, whereas weaker labor markets are tied to completion elsewhere in Minnesota. These effects are consistent but modest compared to demographic and accomplishment-based factors.

::: panel-tabset
#### School Locale (Dem_Desc) → Postsecondary Graduation Location (Ps.grad.location)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 472.32, Cramer’s V = 0.113, p \< .001. Graduation location varied modestly by school locale:

-   **Entirely rural graduates** were more likely to complete outside their region (49.3% elsewhere in MN, 12.6% outside MN). Only 38% stayed within their high school region.
-   **Town/rural mix graduates** were much more likely to stay in-region (55.9%) and less likely to go outside the state (15.4%).
-   **Urban/town/rural mix graduates** were the most likely to remain in-region (61%) and the most likely to graduate outside MN (17.9%), with relatively fewer moving elsewhere in MN (21%).

**Conclusion**:\
School locale shows a modest association with where students graduate from postsecondary institutions. Graduates from entirely rural schools are less likely to remain in-region and more likely to move elsewhere in Minnesota, while graduates from mixed or urban/town/rural schools are more likely to complete within their home region. These findings suggest that regional school context modestly shapes geographic mobility after high school graduation.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad.location,
         var.col = data$Dem_Desc,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad-location/High school characteristics/Dem_Desc.html")
```

#### Average Unemployment Rate → Postsecondary Graduation Location (Ps.grad.location)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(2, 18642) = 19.17, p \< .001.

-   Graduates from **outside Minnesota** came from areas with unemployment rates about 0.19 percentage points lower than those who graduated elsewhere in Minnesota.
-   Graduates from their **home region** also came from areas with unemployment rates about 0.11 percentage points lower than elsewhere-in-MN graduates.
-   Differences between **Region** and **Outside MN** graduates were smaller (0.08 percentage points) but still significant.

**Conclusion**:\
Community unemployment rates are modestly associated with where students complete their postsecondary credentials. Graduates from lower-unemployment areas are somewhat more likely to finish either within their home region or out of state, while those from higher-unemployment areas are more likely to graduate elsewhere in Minnesota. The effects are statistically reliable but small in magnitude.

```{r}
summary <- aov(avg.unemp.rate ~ ps.grad.location, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad-location/High school characteristics/avg-unemp.rate.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)


```

#### Average Wages (as % of Minnesota State Average) → Postsecondary Graduation Location (Ps.grad.location)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(2, 18642) = 107.57, p \< .001.

-   Graduates from **outside Minnesota** came from areas with average wages about 0.012 points higher than those who graduated elsewhere in Minnesota (p \< .001).
-   Graduates from their **home region** came from areas with wages about 0.014 points higher than elsewhere-in-MN graduates (p \< .001).
-   No significant difference emerged between **Region** and **Outside MN** graduates.

**Conclusion**:\
Local wage levels are modestly associated with where students complete postsecondary credentials. Students from higher-wage areas are more likely to graduate either within their home region or outside the state, while students from lower-wage areas are somewhat more likely to graduate elsewhere in Minnesota. The effect, while statistically significant, is relatively small in practical terms.

```{r}
summary <- aov(avg.wages.pct.state ~ ps.grad.location, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad-location/High school characteristics/avg-wage-pct-state.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```
:::

<br>

## High School Accomplishments

**Key Findings**:

-   **ACT Composite Score**: F(2, 14470) = 536.91, p \< .001. Students who graduated elsewhere in Minnesota had the highest ACT scores, while those who graduated in-region or outside Minnesota had lower scores.
-   **PSEO Participation**: Cramer’s V = 0.117, p \< .001. PSEO participants were more geographically mobile—less likely to graduate in-region (54% vs. 66%) and more likely to graduate elsewhere in Minnesota or outside the state.
-   **CTE Achievement**: Cramer’s V = 0.068, p \< .001. CTE concentrators/completors were the most likely to remain in their high school region (65.8%) and the least likely to graduate outside MN (13.2%). Non-CTE students were the least likely to stay local and the most likely to graduate out of state.
-   **Total CTE Courses Taken**: F(2, 18642) = 63.26, p \< .001. Students who stayed in-region had taken the most CTE courses, while those who graduated elsewhere in MN or out of state had taken fewer.
-   **MCA Math**: F(2, 18435) = 204.54, p \< .001. Region graduates had significantly lower math scores than those who graduated elsewhere in MN or out of state.
-   **MCA Reading**: F(2, 18346) = 156.66, p \< .001. Region graduates had significantly lower reading scores than elsewhere-in-MN and out-of-state graduates.
-   **MCA Science**: F(2, 14864) = 236.46, p \< .001. Region graduates had significantly lower science scores than both elsewhere-in-MN and out-of-state graduates.

**Conclusion**:\
High school accomplishments are strongly associated with postsecondary graduation location. **Higher academic achievement (ACT and MCA scores)** is linked to greater geographic mobility, with stronger students more likely to graduate elsewhere in Minnesota or out of state. **PSEO participation** also increases mobility, while **CTE engagement (more courses and concentrator/completor status)** anchors students closer to home, with higher rates of in-region graduation. Overall, academic strength and dual-enrollment experiences encourage mobility, while CTE pathways reinforce regional ties.

::: panel-tabset
#### ACT Composite Score → Postsecondary Graduation Location (Ps.grad.location)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(2, 14470) = 536.91, p \< .001.

-   Students who graduated in their **home region** had ACT scores about 2.39 points lower than those who graduated elsewhere in Minnesota.
-   Students who graduated **outside Minnesota** also had ACT scores about 2.30 points lower than elsewhere-in-MN graduates.
-   No significant difference was observed between Region and Outside-MN graduates.

**Conclusion**:\
ACT scores are strongly associated with where students complete postsecondary credentials. Students with higher ACT scores are more likely to graduate from institutions elsewhere in Minnesota, while those with lower scores tend to remain in their home region or attend out-of-state institutions. The lack of difference between Region and Outside-MN suggests that both represent pathways for students with relatively lower ACT performance compared to those who stay within the state but outside their home region.

```{r}
summary <- aov(ACTCompositeScore ~ ps.grad.location, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad-location/High school accomplishments/ACTCompositeScore.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### PSEO Participation → Postsecondary Graduation Location (Ps.grad.location)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 187.50, Cramer’s V = 0.117, p \< .001:contentReference[oaicite:0]{index="0"}.

-   **Non-PSEO participants** were much more likely to graduate within their high school region (66%) compared to PSEO participants (54%).
-   **PSEO participants** were more likely to graduate elsewhere in Minnesota (27% vs. 20.6%) or outside the state (19% vs. 13.4%).

**Conclusion**:\
PSEO participation is significantly associated with where students graduate. Students who participated in PSEO show greater geographic mobility, more often completing their degrees outside their home region—in other parts of Minnesota or out of state. Non-PSEO students, by contrast, are more likely to remain in their high school region for postsecondary completion.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad.location,
         var.col = data$pseo.participant,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad-location/High school accomplishments/pseo-participant.html")
```

#### CTE Achievement → Postsecondary Graduation Location (Ps.grad.location)

**Key Findings**:\
Chi-square = 171.97, Cramer’s V = 0.068, p \< .001:contentReference[oaicite:0]{index="0"}.

-   **CTE concentrators/completors** were the most likely to remain in their high school region (65.8%) and the least likely to graduate outside MN (13.2%).
-   **CTE participants** were somewhat less likely to stay in-region (58.9%) and more likely to graduate outside MN (16.9%).
-   **Non-CTE students** were the least likely to remain in-region (54.4%) and the most likely to graduate outside MN (19.2%).

**Conclusion**:\
CTE achievement shows a modest but significant association with graduation location. Students with deeper CTE engagement are more likely to stay local and complete within their high school region, while students with little or no CTE involvement demonstrate greater geographic mobility, graduating elsewhere in Minnesota or out of state. This suggests that CTE pathways reinforce regional ties, while non-CTE students pursue more mobile postsecondary trajectories.

```{r}
tab_xtab(var.row = data$ps.grad.location,
         var.col = data$cte.achievement,
         show.exp = TRUE,
         show.col.prc = TRUE,
         file = "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad-location/High school accomplishments/cte-achievement.html")
```

#### Total CTE Courses Taken → Postsecondary Graduation Location (Ps.grad.location)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(2, 18642) = 63.26, p \< .001.

-   Graduates who remained in their **home region** had taken significantly more CTE courses than both elsewhere-in-MN (+0.24 courses) and outside-MN graduates (+0.45 courses).
-   Graduates who completed **outside Minnesota** had taken fewer CTE courses than those in-region (–0.45 courses).
-   The smallest number of CTE courses was observed among students graduating **elsewhere in Minnesota**, who took about 0.21 fewer courses than out-of-state graduates.

**Conclusion**:\
The number of CTE courses taken is significantly related to where students graduate. Students with heavier CTE course loads are more likely to stay in their home region, while those with fewer CTE courses are more likely to graduate outside their region, either elsewhere in Minnesota or out of state. This reinforces the pattern that stronger CTE engagement is tied to local completion, while less CTE involvement aligns with greater geographic mobility.

```{r}
summary <- aov(total.cte.courses.taken ~ ps.grad.location, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad-location/High school accomplishments/total-cte-courses-taken.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### MCA Math Scores → Postsecondary Graduation Location (Ps.grad.location)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(2, 18435) = 204.54, p \< .001.

-   **Region graduates** had significantly lower MCA Math scores than both elsewhere-in-MN graduates (–0.30, p \< .001) and outside-MN graduates (–0.26, p \< .001).
-   No significant difference in Math scores was found between elsewhere-in-MN and outside-MN graduates.

**Conclusion**:\
Math achievement is strongly associated with graduation location. Students with higher MCA Math scores are more likely to graduate outside their home region, whether elsewhere in Minnesota or out of state. Students with lower math scores are more likely to remain in their home region for postsecondary completion, indicating that higher academic achievement is linked to greater geographic mobility.

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.M ~ ps.grad.location, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad-location/High school accomplishments/MCA-M.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```

#### MCA Reading Scores → Postsecondary Graduation Location (Ps.grad.location)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(2, 18346) = 156.66, p \< .001.

-   **Region graduates** had significantly lower MCA Reading scores than both elsewhere-in-MN graduates (–0.19, p \< .001) and outside-MN graduates (–0.18, p \< .001).
-   No significant difference was found between elsewhere-in-MN and outside-MN graduates.

**Conclusion**:\
Reading achievement is strongly associated with graduation location. Students with higher MCA Reading scores are more likely to graduate outside their home region, whether elsewhere in Minnesota or out of state. Students with lower reading scores are more likely to remain in their home region for postsecondary completion, showing a clear link between stronger academic skills and greater geographic mobility.

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.R ~ ps.grad.location, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad-location/High school accomplishments/MCA-R.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)

```

#### MCA Science Scores → Postsecondary Graduation Location (Ps.grad.location)

**Key Findings**:\
ANOVA: F(2, 14864) = 236.46, p \< .001.

-   **Region graduates** had significantly lower MCA Science scores than both elsewhere-in-MN (–0.29, p \< .001) and outside-MN graduates (–0.28, p \< .001).
-   No significant difference was found between elsewhere-in-MN and outside-MN graduates.

**Conclusion**:\
Science achievement is strongly associated with graduation location. Students with higher MCA Science scores are more likely to graduate outside their home region, whether elsewhere in Minnesota or out of state. Students with lower science scores are more likely to remain in their home region, reinforcing the pattern that higher academic achievement is linked with greater geographic mobility.

```{r}
summary <- aov(MCA.S ~ ps.grad.location, data = data)

summary

anova_df <- broom::tidy(summary)

TukeyHSD(summary)

tukey_df <- broom::tidy(TukeyHSD(summary))

wb <- createWorkbook()
addWorksheet(wb, "ANOVA")
addWorksheet(wb, "TukeyHSD")
writeData(wb, "ANOVA", anova_df)
writeData(wb, "TukeyHSD", tukey_df)
saveWorkbook(wb, "Data/ChatGPT/Post-secondary pathways ct/ps-grad-location/High school accomplishments/MCA-S.xlsx", overwrite = TRUE)
```
:::

<br>

# Summary of Postsecondary Pathways Analysis (All Tiers)

This study examined how **demographics, high school characteristics, and high school accomplishments** shape postsecondary pathways across three tiers of outcomes:

1.  **Tier 1: Core Postsecondary Access and Attainment**
    -   Immediate enrollment (attended.ps.within.first.year.hsgrad)\
    -   Ever attended postsecondary (attended.ps)\
    -   Postsecondary graduation (ps.grad)
2.  **Tier 2: Type of Postsecondary Experience**
    -   Highest credential earned (highest.cred.level)
3.  **Tier 3: Location of Postsecondary Experience**
    -   Location of postsecondary graduation (ps.grad.location)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Key Findings by Tier

## Tier 1: Core Access and Attainment

-   **Demographics** were the strongest and most consistent predictors. Economic disadvantage, race/ethnicity (especially American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students), and special education status all reduced likelihood of enrollment, attendance, and completion.
-   **High School Characteristics** (locale, local unemployment, wages) had weak and inconsistent effects.
-   **High School Accomplishments** were powerful predictors. Higher ACT/MCA scores and **PSEO participation** strongly boosted immediate enrollment, persistence, and graduation. In contrast, **greater CTE engagement** was associated with lower enrollment and graduation, aligning with workforce entry rather than postsecondary continuation.

## Tier 2: Type of Postsecondary Experience (Highest Credential Earned)

-   **Economic disadvantage** and **special education status** created the steepest barriers to earning a bachelor’s or higher degree, with these groups concentrated in “Some college” or sub-associate credentials.
-   **Racial disparities** persisted: White and Asian/PI students were far more likely to complete bachelor’s or graduate degrees, while American Indian, Black, and Hispanic students were more likely to stop at associate’s or leave with no credential.
-   **Academic readiness and PSEO participation** were the strongest positive predictors of bachelor’s+ attainment. Students with higher ACT/MCA scores and PSEO experience were much more likely to progress to advanced credentials.
-   **CTE engagement** again tracked with sub-baccalaureate attainment, with concentrators/completors more likely to finish with associate’s or stop at “Some college.”

## Tier 3: Location of Postsecondary Graduation

-   **Demographics shaped mobility**: disadvantaged, American Indian, Hispanic, and special education students were most likely to graduate within their high school region, while non-disadvantaged, White, and Asian/PI students showed greater mobility (elsewhere in MN or out-of-state).
-   **High school characteristics** mattered modestly. Rural students tended to leave their region but stay within Minnesota, while students from stronger labor markets (lower unemployment, higher wages) were more likely to graduate in-region or out-of-state.
-   **Accomplishments mattered most**: Higher ACT/MCA scores and PSEO participation were strongly tied to graduating outside the home region, while heavier CTE engagement (more courses, concentrator/completor status) was associated with staying local.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Overall Interpretation

Across all tiers, the analysis reveals three central themes:

1.  **Structural Inequities Persist**\
    Economic disadvantage, race/ethnicity, and special education status consistently constrain postsecondary success. These students are less likely to enroll immediately, less likely to persist, less likely to graduate, and when they do, they are more likely to complete at lower credential levels and closer to home.

2.  **Academic Readiness and PSEO are Game-Changers**\
    ACT and MCA scores—especially in reading and science—are among the strongest predictors of bachelor’s and graduate attainment, while **PSEO participation stands out as the single most effective programmatic lever**, doubling the likelihood of bachelor’s+ completion and encouraging geographic mobility.

3.  **CTE Pathways Support Local, Sub-Baccalaureate Outcomes**\
    Intensive CTE engagement aligns strongly with associate-level attainment and local graduation. While CTE provides clear workforce pathways, it appears less effective in propelling students toward bachelor’s or graduate degrees, reflecting its different role in student trajectories.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Conclusion

The analysis underscores that **postsecondary pathways are not just about whether students attend, but how, where, and how far they progress.**

-   **Equity gaps** remain entrenched, especially for disadvantaged, racially marginalized, and special education students.
-   **Academic preparation and dual-enrollment opportunities** (like PSEO) dramatically expand access to higher-level and geographically broader attainment.
-   **CTE pathways** anchor students locally and at sub-baccalaureate levels, serving as important workforce pipelines but not drivers of higher educational attainment.

Ultimately, policies and practices that strengthen academic readiness, expand access to PSEO, and address demographic inequities will be most effective in raising not only participation and completion rates but also the **level and reach of postsecondary success**.
